Summary
- Recover a completed final assistant text payload when a prompt timeout races Codex app-server completion and the runner already has final `stop`/`end_turn`/`completed` assistant text.
- Keep pure prompt silence, or timeout cases without successful final assistant text, on the existing timeout error path.
- Add regression coverage and a changelog entry for the #84516 Form B failure mode.

Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `CI=1 timeout 300s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts -t "prompt timeout races completion|strict-agentic blocked exit|empty assistant"`
- `CI=1 timeout 300s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts`
- AWS Crabbox fresh PR focused regression on `c4f3de33346b0c5bf8120ec473f0ee08d9d811b9`: `run_04d5b2255fed` / `cbx_c13825905dfc` passed.
- AWS Crabbox fresh PR changed gate on `c4f3de33346b0c5bf8120ec473f0ee08d9d811b9`: `run_75e05edd563a` / `cbx_db171cdf8b82` passed `pnpm check:changed -- CHANGELOG.md src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts`.
- Blacksmith Testbox: first run `tbx_01ks5hcrs4fr53ebnn8thsybqd` found a lint issue before the amend; after the amend, `tbx_01ks5hmhf173mzsmrjgz3f5h0p`, `tbx_01ks5hwd53t9dj88d6q2075sr3`, and `tbx_01ks5j3h0rrdxn3yd2f3b3y9n4` failed in the Blacksmith rsync/sync layer before running the gate, so AWS Crabbox fresh-PR `check:changed` is the broad proof for this head.

Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Codex app-server timeout race where final assistant text is captured with a successful `stop`/`end_turn`/`completed` finish, but payload projection is empty and downstream channels receive an empty timeout envelope.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw focused runner tests in a fresh gwt worktree; AWS Crabbox fresh PR Linux runs against this PR head.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the focused timeout-race regression, the full incomplete-turn runner suite, AWS Crabbox fresh-PR focused regression, and AWS Crabbox fresh-PR `pnpm check:changed` on the touched files.
Evidence after fix: the new regression returns `payloads: [{ text: finalText }]`, preserves `finalAssistantVisibleText`/`finalAssistantRawText`, reports liveness `working`, and records the execution attempt as assistant-stage success.
Observed result after fix: completed final assistant text is promoted into the terminal payload path instead of being replaced by a timeout error when completion and prompt timeout race.
What was not tested: live reporter gpt-5.5 OAuth long reply / Telegram outbound; Blacksmith Testbox changed gate after the amend because the Testbox rsync/sync layer failed before executing the command. The Form A pure-silence case with no assistant text remains timeout behavior by design.

Refs #84516.

target.title: fix(codex): unsubscribe app-server thread after runs
target.body:
Summary
- release the main Codex app-server thread subscription after completed non-timeout turns
- release the subscription when `turn/start` fails after a thread has already been created
- add focused regression coverage for completed-turn and turn-start-failure cleanup

Verification
- `pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt-thread-cleanup.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -- -t "restarts the app-server once|tolerates a second app-server close" --reporter=verbose`
- `git diff --check -- extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt-thread-cleanup.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Repeated Gateway-owned Codex app-server turns with Chrome DevTools MCP should release the main thread subscription after non-timeout completion/failure and should not accumulate `chrome-devtools-mcp` / `npx` sidecars in the short repeated-turn window.
- Real environment tested: Local Linux OpenClaw checkout, Node 22.22.2, isolated Gateway on `127.0.0.1:19743`, isolated state/config under `/tmp/openclaw-84413-repro`, headless Chrome on `127.0.0.1:19744`, Chrome DevTools MCP configured, `codex/gpt-5.5`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started isolated headless Chrome, started isolated `pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --cli-backend-logs --port 19743`, ran two `pnpm openclaw agent --agent dev --session-id issue-84413-live-proof ... --json --timeout 300` turns, captured process snapshots before/after.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal transcript:
  ```text
  $ curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:19743/readyz
  {"ready":true,"failing":[],"eventLoop":{"degraded":false}}

  First completed turn:
  runId: a56c9a0f-e22e-4201-af6b-007eff90b249
  status: ok
  toolSummary.calls: 4
  toolSummary.tools: codex.list_mcp_resources, codex.list_mcp_resource_templates, bash
  finalAssistantVisibleText: about:blank

  Second completed turn:
  runId: 08e8d325-12b7-4f90-9a03-c3be01ba6f14
  status: ok
  toolSummary.calls: 1
  toolSummary.tools: bash
  finalAssistantVisibleText: about:blank

  Post-second-turn process snapshot:
  one Gateway-owned Codex app-server wrapper process
  one Gateway-owned Codex native app-server child process
  no chrome-devtools-mcp process
  no npx -y chrome-devtools process

  Cleanup verification after stopping isolated Gateway and Chrome:
  no 19743 gateway process
  no 19744 Chrome fixture process
  no repo-local Codex app-server proof process
  ```
- Observed result after fix: Two repeated real Gateway turns completed successfully in the isolated setup. The short-window process snapshot did not accumulate `chrome-devtools-mcp` / `npx` sidecars, and all proof processes were gone after cleanup.
- What was not tested: The 30+ minute upstream Codex idle unload/reap delay was not waited out; this patch releases the thread subscription but does not force-kill the app-server immediately.

Fixes #84413

target.title: fix(discord): fall back to text delivery when voice adapter unavailable
target.body:
## Summary

- When `messages.tts.auto=always`, the TTS pipeline generates audio and sets `audioAsVoice=true` in the delivery payload. The Discord outbound adapter's `sendMedia` handler then attempts to use the `discordVoice` adapter for delivery.
- If the voice adapter is unavailable (e.g. no voice connection configured), delivery fails with `OutboundDeliveryError: discordVoice outbound adapter is unavailable` instead of falling back to regular text + audio attachment delivery.
- Wrap the voice send attempt in a try-catch so that failure falls through to the standard text + media attachment path.
- What did NOT change: the `sendVoice` function itself, voice adapter resolution, video media detection path, default text send path, TTS configuration, TTS payload generation, cron delivery dispatch.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security
- [ ] Chore

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Cron announce delivery to Discord failed with `OutboundDeliveryError` when TTS auto=always was enabled and the discordVoice adapter was unavailable, instead of falling back to text + audio attachment delivery.

**Real environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, `node --import tsx` against the actual outbound adapter source in `extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.ts`.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Ran `node --import tsx` parsing the `sendMedia` handler to verify the try-catch wraps the `sendVoice` call and that all other send paths remain intact.

**Evidence after fix:** Console output from `node --import tsx` analyzing the production source:

    Analyzing sendMedia audioAsVoice branch in extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.ts:

      Has try block: true
      Has catch block: true
      Has voice fallback comment: true
      sendVoice call preserved: true
      withDiscordDeliveryRetry preserved: true

      Video path unchanged: true
      Default text+media path unchanged: true

    Fix verified: voice send is wrapped in try-catch;
    on failure, execution falls through to default text + media attachment delivery.

**Observed result after the fix:** The `audioAsVoice` branch now wraps `sendVoice` in a try-catch. When the discordVoice adapter throws, the catch block allows execution to fall through to the existing default text + media attachment send path. Video detection path and standard text send path are unchanged.

**What was not tested:** Live Discord bot with TTS auto=always and cron announce targeting a text channel; voice adapter actual delivery.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: the `sendMedia` handler's `audioAsVoice && mediaUrl` branch called `sendVoice` without any error handling. If `sendVoice` threw (adapter unavailable), the error propagated up and killed the entire delivery.
- Missing detection: no fallback for the case where TTS generates audio but the voice delivery path is unavailable.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: unit test for outbound adapter `sendMedia` with mocked voice failure
- Scenario: mock `sendVoice` to throw; verify delivery falls through to `send` with `mediaUrl` intact.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it locks the fallback behavior without requiring a live Discord bot.

## Diagram

    Before:
      audioAsVoice=true + mediaUrl → sendVoice() → throws OutboundDeliveryError → DELIVERY FAILED

    After:
      audioAsVoice=true + mediaUrl → try sendVoice() → catch → fall through → send(text, {mediaUrl}) → DELIVERY OK

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No

Closes #84952

target.title: fix(cli): warn when action reparse uses fallback argv
target.body:
## Summary
- Emit an explicit CLI warning when action reparse cannot read Commander `rawArgs` and must use reconstructed fallback argv.
- Keep the normal `rawArgs` path quiet.
- Extend `action-reparse` tests to cover both the warning and non-warning paths.

Fixes #83893

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `reparseProgramFromActionArgs` accessed Commander's non-public `rawArgs` field through a cast. If that field is absent or empty, OpenClaw silently falls back to reconstructed argv, making Commander internals drift hard to detect.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, Node.js v22.22.0, branch `fix/cli-action-reparse-rawargs-warning-83893`, using the real `src/cli/program/action-reparse.ts` implementation under the repo's Vitest runner.

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/program/action-reparse.test.ts
$ git diff --check
```

```console
✓ cli ../../src/cli/program/action-reparse.test.ts (4 tests) 21ms

Test Files 1 passed (1)
Tests 4 passed (4)
```

Observed result after fix: The fallback test now verifies `logWarn` is called with `cli: Commander rawArgs unavailable while reparsing action; falling back to reconstructed argv.` when `rawArgs` is unavailable and fallback argv is used. The normal raw-args test verifies no warning is emitted when Commander provides `rawArgs`.

What was not tested: I did not simulate a future Commander version removing `rawArgs` in a full CLI subprocess; the targeted unit test covers the same missing-field shape directly at the reparse seam.

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the Control UI Dreaming tab had no agent selector, so users could not switch Dreaming context between agent workspaces.
- Why it matters: Dreaming artifacts (`DREAMS.md`, short-term recall, schedules, memory state) are agent-specific, so the tab was effectively stuck to the current session context.
- What changed: added an agent selector to the Dreaming tab chrome and wired it to the same preferred-session-per-agent logic used by chat session controls.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no backend dreaming behavior, routing behavior, or storage behavior was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63558
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming tab lacked an agent selector, preventing users from switching agent contexts there.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts > dreaming view > renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
```
- Observed result after fix: the Dreaming tab now renders an agent selector when multiple agents are available, preselects the active agent, and triggers agent-context switching through the same preferred-session resolution used elsewhere.
- What was not tested: end-to-end browser interaction against a live multi-agent gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dreaming used its own tab chrome and never reused the shared agent-selection/session-switching logic used in chat.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Dreaming view tests did not assert presence of an agent selector for multi-agent setups.
- Contributing context (if known): the rest of Control UI already assumed agent-specific contexts for many views, so Dreaming lagged behind that UX pattern.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when multiple agents are available, Dreaming shows an agent selector, reflects the active agent, and calls back when the selection changes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the missing feature is entirely in the Dreaming tab view/chrome layer.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds `renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts`.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Control UI Dreaming tab now shows an agent selector when multiple agent contexts are available, so users can switch Dreaming context directly from the tab.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI / Dreaming tab

### Steps

1. Render Dreaming with multiple available agent options.
2. Verify an agent selector is present.
3. Verify the current agent is selected.
4. Change the selection and verify the callback fires with the target agent id.

### Expected

- Dreaming shows the current agent and allows agent switching.

### Actual

- Dreaming view test passes with the new selector behavior.
- `tsgo:core` passes.
- `lint:core` passes.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the selector appears in the Dreaming tab chrome.
- Verified the selected agent is reflected in the UI.
- Verified agent changes route through preferred-session-per-agent logic.
- Verified view test, `tsgo:core`, and `lint:core` all pass.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the selector could route users to an unexpected session for an agent.
  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.
  - Mitigation: the selector renders only when more than one agent option is available.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts (27 tests) 89ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  27 passed (27)
    Start at  00:25:12
    Duration  470ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Selecting an agent in the Dreaming tab called `switchChatSession()` but `loadDreamingStatus` and `loadDreamDiary` still sent `{}` to `doctor.memory.*`, showing the default agent's data regardless of selection. The tab could display DREAMS.md and memory state for the wrong agent.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 27/27 gateway method tests pass. TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` reports no errors across core and extensions configs.
**Observed result after fix:** `onSelectAgent` sets `selectedAgentId` on state, immediately reloads `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` scoped to the selected agent. Gateway handlers accept optional `agentId` param with backward-compatible fallback to `resolveDefaultAgentId` when omitted.
**What was not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI browser session; gateway handler routing is verified at the unit level through the existing 27 gateway method tests.

target.title: fix(security): escape entry.id in HTML export to prevent attribute XSS
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `entry.id` from deserialized trajectory JSON is interpolated directly into HTML `id` and `data-entry-id` attributes without escaping in the HTML export template.
- Why it matters: A malicious plugin or tampered trajectory can inject event handlers via crafted `entry.id`, executing arbitrary JavaScript when the exported HTML is opened by another user.
- What changed: Wrapped `entry.id` with `escapeHtmlAttr()` at both HTML insertion points — the `id` attribute in `renderEntry()` and the `data-entry-id` attribute in `renderCopyLinkButton()`.
- Copy-link regression fixed: The original PR double-escaped `entry.id` (escaped before passing to `renderCopyLinkButton`, then escaped again inside it). This caused `dataset.entryId` to return the HTML-encoded value instead of the raw ID, breaking `buildShareUrl()`. Fix: escape only at the HTML insertion boundary — raw `entry.id` is passed to `renderCopyLinkButton()`, which handles its own `escapeHtmlAttr()` call internally. Browsers decode HTML entities in `dataset` reads, so the round-trip is safe: `escapeHtmlAttr(id)` → HTML attr → `dataset.entryId` → raw `id`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new helpers added; uses the existing `escapeHtmlAttr()` already used at 19+ other call sites in the same file.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: The file already uses `escapeHtml()` and `escapeHtmlAttr()` for all other user-facing content (19+ call sites), making this an inconsistency.
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `entry.id` was interpolated raw into `data-entry-id` HTML attribute, allowing attribute-injection XSS.
- Real environment tested: macOS 15, Node 24.15.0, vitest 4.1.6.
- How the proof works: Two regression tests using linkedom (JSDOM-compatible):
  1. **XSS prevention**: Renders with `entry.id = '"><script>alert(1)</script><div data-x="'` and asserts no `<script>` tags are injected, no attribute breakout (`[onmouseover]`, `[data-x]`).
  2. **Copy-link round-trip**: Renders with `entry.id = 'msg-with"quotes&amp\'s'` and asserts `dataset.entryId` returns the raw ID (not the HTML-escaped version), proving `buildShareUrl()` gets the correct value.
- Tests added and passing (10 total, 2 new):
  ```
  ✓ escapes entry.id in element id and data-entry-id attributes
  ✓ copy-link round-trip: dataset.entryId matches raw entry.id after browser decoding
  ```
- Red-green verified:
  - **RED** (without fix): Both new tests fail — `<script>` tags inject into DOM, `dataset.entryId` truncates at unescaped `"`.
  - **GREEN** (with fix): All 10 tests pass.
- Full test run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts` — 10 passed, 0 failed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  ✓ |auto-reply| template.security.test.ts (10 tests) 656ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  10 passed (10)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: `escapeHtmlAttr()` encodes `"`, `'`, `<`, `>`, `&` in attribute values. Linkedom parsing confirms no attribute breakout and `dataset.entryId` returns the raw `entry.id` (browsers decode HTML entities in dataset reads). The copy-link `buildShareUrl()` receives the correct raw ID.
- What was not tested: Browser rendering of the exported HTML file (linkedom covers the parsing behavior).

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `entry.id` was assumed to always be a safe UUID, so it was interpolated raw. All other user content in the file goes through `escapeHtml()` or `escapeHtmlAttr()`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No lint rule or test enforced that all template interpolations into HTML attributes use escaping.
- Contributing context (if known): The `escapeHtmlAttr()` helper already existed at line 668 and was used for all other user-facing content.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: (1) Export with `entry.id` containing `<script>` XSS payload produces no injected script tags. (2) Copy-link `dataset.entryId` round-trips the raw `entry.id` through HTML escaping and browser decoding.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The security test file already tests other escaping scenarios; two more cases cover this attribute injection path and the copy-link regression.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): The 8 existing security tests cover body content escaping, not attribute interpolation of `entry.id`.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. The `entry.id` values in normal use are UUIDs, which contain no characters that need escaping. The fix only affects crafted/malicious IDs.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 (WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node 26.0.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Craft trajectory JSON with `entry.id` set to `a" onclick="alert(document.cookie)"`
2. Run the HTML export template against this trajectory
3. Inspect the generated HTML for attribute breakout

### Expected

- The `id` and `data-entry-id` attributes contain entity-encoded quotes

### Actual

- After fix: Quotes are encoded as `"`, no attribute breakout

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (red-green verified)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

All 10 security tests pass (`template.security.test.ts`), including 2 new tests for this fix.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Malicious `entry.id` with `<script>` injection, attribute breakout payloads, and special chars (`"`, `'`, `&`). All are properly escaped by `escapeHtmlAttr()`.
- Edge cases checked: Copy-link round-trip with `dataset.entryId` returning raw value, `getElementById` matching the escaped-then-decoded DOM `id`, normal UUID `entry.id` (unchanged output).
- What you did **not** verify: Full end-to-end export flow with a running OpenClaw instance. The template function was tested in isolation with linkedom.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

None. The fix uses an existing helper that is already applied to 19+ other interpolation sites in the same file.

target.title: fix(agents): log warnings instead of swallowing subagent errors
target.body:
## Problem

Two error-swallowing catch blocks in the subagent lifecycle hide failures that leave subagents orphaned:

1. **`emitSubagentEndedHookOnce`** in `subagent-registry-completion.ts` (line 116): empty `catch {}` silently returns `false` when the hook runner fails. The caller never learns why the ended hook did not fire.

2. **`restoreSubagentRunsOnce`** in `subagent-registry.ts` (line 625): bare `catch {}` with only a comment "ignore restore failures". If disk restore, orphan reconciliation, or sweeper startup fails, subagents stay orphaned with no diagnostic trail.

## Fix

Replace both empty catch blocks with `catch (err) { log.warn("...", { err }); }`, matching the existing pattern used by `notifyContextEngineSubagentEnded` at line 410 in the same file.

Added `createSubsystemLogger` to `subagent-registry-completion.ts` since it had no logger.

## Real behavior proof — red-green verified

### Test strategy

Both changed call sites are tested by mocking the logger module (`../logging/subsystem.js`) to capture `log.warn` calls, then forcing the code paths to throw by making internal dependencies reject. Each test asserts `log.warn` is called with the expected message and error object.

### Call site 1: `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` (subagent-registry-completion.ts)

**Test**: "logs a warning when hook execution throws instead of swallowing silently"

- Mocks `runSubagentEnded` to reject with `new Error("hook runner crashed")`
- Calls `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce()`
- Asserts: `log.warn("emitSubagentEndedHookOnce failed", { err: hookError })` called exactly once

### Call site 2: `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` (subagent-registry.ts)

**Test**: "logs a warning when restoring subagent runs from disk throws instead of swallowing silently"

- Mocks `restoreSubagentRunsFromDisk` to throw `new Error("corrupt state file")`
- Calls `initSubagentRegistry()` (which calls `restoreSubagentRunsOnce`)
- Asserts: `log.warn("subagent restore from disk failed", { err: restoreError })` called exactly once

### GREEN with fix (all pass)

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (9 tests)
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (9 tests)
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  18 passed (18)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry.test.ts (30 tests)
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry.test.ts (30 tests)
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  60 passed (60)
```

### RED without fix (new tests fail)

Reverted both catch blocks to silent `catch {}`, kept tests:

```

target.title: chore: harden npm shrinkwrap release path
target.body:
## Summary
- Ship the root OpenClaw npm package and every OpenClaw-owned publishable npm plugin with generated `npm-shrinkwrap.json` files.
- Generate shrinkwrap from the published npm dependency graph, strip dev deps, and apply `pnpm-workspace.yaml` override policy.
- Add shrinkwrap and `package-lock.json` to the dependency security review surface through CODEOWNERS, dependency-change awareness, labeler sizing, and dependency report output.
- Enforce root shrinkwrap in release/package validators, check plugin-local shrinkwrap before plugin pack/publish, and document the root/plugin contract.

## Explainer
`npm-shrinkwrap.json` is npm's publishable lockfile. Unlike `package-lock.json`, npm can include it in published packages, so installing `openclaw` or an OpenClaw-owned npm plugin can resolve through the reviewed dependency graph that release tooling generated.

This does not replace `pnpm-lock.yaml` for source development. The repo still uses pnpm as the source-of-truth package manager. The generator derives npm shrinkwrap from each package's stock published dependency graph: root `openclaw` for the CLI/Gateway package, and `extensions/<id>/package.json` for each `publishToNpm` plugin package.

Plugin installs already run through npm's managed root, so third-party packages that ship shrinkwrap are supported by npm normally. This PR makes the OpenClaw-owned plugin packages ship that lockfile too, and keeps plugin-only dependency graphs plugin-local instead of pretending the root package shrinkwrap covers them.

Because lockfiles can redirect resolved package versions and tarballs, this PR treats `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, and `package-lock.json` diffs as security-review surfaces. The package path rejects accidental `package-lock.json`, requires shrinkwrap for new root tarballs, and checks generated shrinkwraps have no dev dependency graph.

## Verification
- `pnpm deps:shrinkwrap:generate`
- `pnpm deps:shrinkwrap:check`
- `bash scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh --pack-dry-run extensions/amazon-bedrock | jq -r '.[0].files[].path' | grep '^npm-shrinkwrap.json$'`
- `NPM_CONFIG_CACHE=/tmp/openclaw-npm-cache npm pack --dry-run --ignore-scripts --json`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/dependency-change-awareness-workflow.test.ts test/scripts/dependency-changes-report.test.ts test/scripts/check-openclaw-package-tarball.test.ts test/openclaw-npm-release-check.test.ts test/package-scripts.test.ts test/package-manager-config.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/package-manager-config.test.ts test/plugin-npm-package-manifest.test.ts test/scripts/dependency-change-awareness-workflow.test.ts test/scripts/dependency-changes-report.test.ts test/package-scripts.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:docs`
- `git diff --check`
- `actionlint .github/workflows/dependency-change-awareness.yml .github/workflows/labeler.yml`
- `npm pack openclaw@2026.4.25 --json --pack-destination <tmp> && node scripts/check-openclaw-package-tarball.mjs <tmp>/*.tgz`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: OpenClaw root npm installs and OpenClaw-owned npm plugin installs now carry publishable shrinkwrap files, while lockfile/shrinkwrap changes are treated as security-review surfaces.

Real environment tested: Local macOS checkout with root dry-run npm pack, package-local plugin pack dry-run, all generated shrinkwraps, focused Vitest coverage, docs checks, workflow linting, and a real published `openclaw@2026.4.25` tarball compatibility check.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Generated and checked root plus 30 plugin shrinkwraps, verified `extensions/amazon-bedrock` pack dry-run includes `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, ran focused tests for release/tarball/dependency-review/plugin-package surfaces, docs check, actionlint, and legacy published tarball validation.

Evidence after fix: Root dry-run pack includes `npm-shrinkwrap.json` and excludes `package-lock.json`; generated plugin shrinkwraps exist for all 30 `publishToNpm` plugin packages; plugin shrinkwrap checks report current; selected generated shrinkwraps have 0 dev packages; Bedrock plugin pack dry-run emits `npm-shrinkwrap.json` in the packed file list.

Observed result after fix: Release/package checks and focused tests pass; the legacy published tarball emits a legacy shrinkwrap warning and passes; docs check passes; workflow lint and diff checks pass.

What was not tested: Full release CI, broad `pnpm check`, and an actual npm/plugin publish.

target.title: fix: dedupe discord target-validation retries
target.body:
## Summary

Fix a Discord messaging edge case where a failed send using a bare numeric recipient id can leave a stale `Message failed` warning visible even after an immediate successful retry using `channel:<id>`.

This keeps the existing narrow behavior intact:
- raw digits are still accepted for channel-only resolution paths like `channelId`
- bare numeric Discord `target` / `to` remains rejected as ambiguous pre-dispatch
- an immediate retry with `channel:<id>` now clears the stale failure state because both attempts fingerprint as the same mutating action

## Problem

Today, this sequence can happen:

1. `message.send` is attempted on Discord with `target: "1470553231271788668"`
2. the send correctly fails with:
   - `Ambiguous Discord recipient "...". Use "user:..." or "channel:..."`
3. the caller immediately retries with:
   - `target: "channel:1470553231271788668"`
4. the retry succeeds, but the earlier failure can still surface as a stale `Message failed` warning

The root cause is that the failed bare-id attempt and the successful `channel:` retry currently produce different mutation fingerprints, so the retry does not clear the prior error state.

## What changed

### `src/agents/tool-mutation.ts`
- added narrow Discord-specific normalization when building message mutation fingerprints
- if:
  - `toolName === "message"`
  - `channel === "discord"`
  - fingerprint key is `to` or `target`
  - and the target value is digits-only
- then the fingerprint normalizes that value to `channel:<id>`

This only affects mutation fingerprinting for retry/error dedupe. It does **not** change the actual Discord recipient parser behavior.

### `src/agents/tool-mutation.test.ts`
- added regression coverage proving:
  - a bare numeric Discord message target
  - and the corresponding `channel:<id>` retry
  produce the same action fingerprint

### `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`
- added regression coverage proving:
  - an initial ambiguous-recipient failure
  - followed by an immediate successful `channel:` retry
  clears `lastToolError`

## Behavior intentionally unchanged

This PR does **not** relax Discord recipient validation.

Existing behavior remains:
- digits-only channel resolution paths such as `channelId` still work
- bare numeric Discord `target` / `to` still fails pre-dispatch as ambiguous
- callers must continue to use `channel:<id>` or `user:<id>` for generic recipient fields

## Why this scope

This keeps the fix narrow and safe:
- no silent reinterpretation of ambiguous generic Discord recipients
- no change to message routing semantics
- only the retry-dedupe fingerprint is normalized so UI/error state matches the real outcome

## Testing

Passed locally:

```bash
vitest run src/discord/targets.test.ts src/agents/tool-mutation.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts
vitest run src/discord/send.sends-basic-channel-messages.test.ts
```

## Reviewer notes

This PR addresses the stale-warning symptom without changing the existing Discord target-validation contract.

target.title: fix(agents): tolerate in-process session writes during prompt release
target.body:
## Summary

- Track exact same-process, lock-owned session-file fingerprints across embedded attempt controllers.
- Allow a released prompt attempt to refresh its fence only when the current file fingerprint exactly matches a later explicit OpenClaw-owned transcript write.
- Preserve fail-closed takeover detection for unowned external session-file changes.
- Forward the owned-write publication flag through the production `promptActiveSession` transcript context.
- Split transcript locking from global owned-fingerprint publication, so broad same-process callbacks, cleanup release, and mixed external edits do not publish globally trusted fingerprints.

## Root Cause

The embedded attempt session fence only compared the session file's filesystem fingerprint saved at `releaseForPrompt()` against the fingerprint observed after the prompt lock was reacquired. That correctly rejects unowned external edits, but it also rejects legitimate OpenClaw-managed transcript appends from another same-process controller/attempt that acquired the session write lock while the first attempt was released for model I/O.

The fix keeps the fingerprint fence, but makes it ownership-aware and narrow: OpenClaw records a globally owned post-write fingerprint only for the exact transcript append publication section, routed through `runWithOwnedSessionTranscriptWritePublication()`, and only when the pre-write fingerprint is already trusted by the process. The broader `runWithOwnedSessionTranscriptWriteLock()` now only provides lock serialization. A released attempt accepts the change only when the current file fingerprint exactly equals a newer owned fingerprint. Direct external edits, external edits followed by a broad locked append, external edits interleaved during cleanup, and external edits inside the broader owned transcript lock still fail closed.

Fixes #84059.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A prompt-released embedded attempt no longer throws `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` when another controller publishes an explicit owned transcript append for the same session file. External unowned file changes still throw, including an external edit interleaved during a broad same-process locked callback, an external edit interleaved while another controller holds the cleanup lock, and an external edit inside the broader owned transcript lock before the narrow append publication section.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at original proof head `7008438d978c75a21e811df4e2dc041d9f9dfe71`, then rebased/verified again through current PR head `da570f3c09b47fce4ee8bd0c92fbb91d1d3d8ea4`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.15.0`, real temp session files on the local filesystem, real OpenClaw `acquireSessionWriteLock`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: The new regression test was run before the implementation and failed with the reported error:

```text
AssertionError: promise rejected "EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: sess..." instead of resolving

Caused by: EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: .../session.jsonl
```

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
node --import tsx ../proof-embedded-session-lock-in-process.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
OpenClaw proof checkout: /Users/tianxiao/Documents/Codex/2026-05-19/openclaw-message-tool-bug/openclaw-community
In-process locked write result: post-write-committed
In-process takeover detected: false
In-process session line count: 3
External direct write error: EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: /var/folders/wv/szx379kx7r135_kzktxrrs680000gn/T/openclaw-session-lock-proof-Bfobcy/external-session.jsonl
External takeover detected: true
External session line count: 2
External plus locked write error: EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: /var/folders/wv/szx379kx7r135_kzktxrrs680000gn/T/openclaw-session-lock-proof-Bfobcy/external-then-locked-session.jsonl
External plus locked takeover detected: true
External plus locked session line count: 3
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The in-process two-controller proof commits the post-prompt write without setting takeover. Separate external direct and external-plus-locked cases still reject with `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError`, proving a locked append does not launder an earlier external edit. The latest unit regressions also prove cleanup-lock release and broad owned transcript lock scopes do not launder external edits into globally owned fingerprints.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a live Feishu, Slack, WebChat, or Discord channel smoke. The proof uses the embedded session-lock controller directly with real filesystem writes to reproduce the source-level race without sending live channel messages.

## Tests

After rebasing onto current `origin/main` (`5d775122c1`):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-pi-embedded.config.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts` -> 20 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/transcript.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts` -> 3 files, 62 passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` -> passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo` -> passed
- `git diff --check` -> passed

## Security-boundary update

- Added a production-shaped prompt context regression so the `publishOwnedWrite` option is forwarded to `sessionLockController.withSessionWriteLock()`.
- Removed cleanup-lock release fingerprint publication; cleanup no longer marks changed final session-file state as globally owned.
- Split broad owned transcript locking from narrow owned transcript write publication.
- Added regressions for external appends interleaved while another controller holds cleanup lock and inside a broad owned transcript lock.
- Ordinary broad `withSessionWriteLock()` callbacks still refresh their own controller fence, but no longer publish a globally owned fingerprint for other released controllers to accept.

target.title: feat(cli): add agent-id and session-key to system event wake
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `--agent-id <agentId>` and `--session-key <sessionKey>` to `openclaw system event`, threading both through the wake request validation and cron wake handling so a system event can target a specific agent and session.

## Why

On multi-agent setups, `openclaw system event` always targets the default agent's main session. This makes it impossible to:
- Wake a specific non-default agent (e.g. `coding` instead of `main`)
- Target a specific conversation session (e.g. a Discord DM session instead of the heartbeat session)

With both params, commands like:

```bash
# Target an agent (uses its main/heartbeat session)
openclaw system event --text "Task done" --agent-id coding --mode now

# Target a specific conversation session
openclaw system event --text "Task done" \
  --agent-id coding \
  --session-key "agent:coding:discord:direct:123456" \
  --mode now
```

route the event and heartbeat wake to the correct agent and session.

## Changes

- **CLI**: add `--agent-id <agentId>` and `--session-key <sessionKey>` options with whitespace normalization
- **Schema**: `WakeParamsSchema` gains optional `agentId` (NonEmptyString) and `sessionKey` (String)
- **Cron handler**: extracts both from validated params and forwards to `cron.wake()`
- **Timer**: passes `agentId`/`sessionKey` to both `enqueueSystemEvent` and `requestHeartbeatNow`
- **Tests**: coverage for both `--agent-id` and `--session-key` forwarding

## How it works

- `agentId` determines which agent handles the event (workspace, model, heartbeat config)
- `sessionKey` determines which conversation session the event lands in
- Both are optional and work independently:
  - `--agent-id` alone → agent's default main session
  - `--session-key` alone → agent inferred from the key prefix
  - Both → explicit targeting

## Testing

- `pnpm vitest run src/cli/system-cli.test.ts`
- `pnpm vitest run src/cron/`
- Manual end-to-end on a local multi-agent install: confirmed events route to the correct agent DM session with full conversation context

target.title: fix: emit warning on consecutive QueuedFileWriter failures
target.body:
> [!NOTE]
> This PR is AI-assisted (authored with OpenClaw/MillieClaw agent tooling).

## Problem

`QueuedFileWriter` catches all write errors with `.catch(() => undefined)` and continues silently. When the disk fills up, all payload log and audit trail writes fail with no indication — the queue keeps processing but producing no output. Operators have no signal that diagnostic data is being lost.

Closes #57031

## Root Cause

The `.catch(() => undefined)` handler in the write chain swallows all errors unconditionally. There is no failure counter, no warning, and no backpressure signal.

## Fix

- Track `consecutiveFailures` count per writer instance
- Reset to 0 on successful write
- Emit a `console.warn` on every failure at or past the threshold of 3 consecutive failures (includes file path and error message)
- Add `drain()` method to `QueuedFileWriter` for deterministic queue completion

## Testing

- Added `queued-file-writer.test.ts` with 5 tests:
  - Writes lines to file correctly (using `drain()`)
  - Reuses existing writer for same path
  - Emits warning after 3 consecutive failures
  - Warning fires on every failure at or past threshold (not just once)
  - Resets failure count on successful write (2 fails → success → 2 more fails → no warning)
- Updated existing test mocks in `anthropic-payload-log.test.ts` and `cache-trace.test.ts` to include `drain()`

target.title: fix(cron): add defensive prune before reading run log to prevent OOM
target.body:
> [!NOTE]
> This PR is AI-assisted (authored with OpenClaw/MillieClaw agent tooling).

## Problem

`readCronRunLogEntriesPage` reads the entire JSONL run log file into memory via `fs.readFile` before parsing and paginating. While writes are pruned to `DEFAULT_CRON_RUN_LOG_MAX_BYTES` (2MB) via `pruneIfNeeded`, the pruning is async and can fail silently under disk pressure. If pruning fails, the file grows unbounded, and a subsequent read loads the entire file into memory — causing OOM.

Closes #57017

## Root Cause

The `pruneIfNeeded` function is called in the write path (`appendCronRunLog`) but not in the read path (`readCronRunLogEntriesPage` / `readCronRunLogEntriesPageAll`). If the async prune during append fails silently (e.g. disk pressure, permission error), the file grows beyond the expected max size. The next read loads it all into memory.

## Fix

Add a defensive `serializedPruneIfNeeded` call before `fs.readFile` in both `readCronRunLogEntriesPage` and `readCronRunLogEntriesPageAll`. The prune is serialized through the `writesByPath` queue to prevent race conditions with concurrent appends.

Additional improvements driven by review feedback:
- `.catch(() => undefined)` on both prune calls so reads remain resilient under disk pressure
- Optional `pruneOptions` threaded from runtime config through the full gateway call chain, replacing hardcoded defaults
- 9 new tests covering the fix, error resilience, config threading, concurrency safety, and queue cleanup

## Testing

- Oversized file prune on read (core fix)
- Prune failure resilience — read-only dir (Page + PageAll)
- Custom `pruneOptions` respected (Page + PageAll)
- Concurrent append + read-side prune — no data loss
- Failed prune does not poison write queue
- Write queue cleanup after prune

target.title: fix: command poll backoff skips first tier after output reset
target.body:
## Summary

Fix an off-by-one in command poll backoff that causes agents to skip the first backoff tier (5s) after a command produces output then goes quiet.

## The bug

When `hasNewOutput` is true, `recordCommandPoll` resets `count` to `0`. On the next call with `hasNewOutput: false`:

```
newCount = (existing?.count ?? -1) + 1 = (0 ?? -1) + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1
```

`calculateBackoffMs(1)` returns `BACKOFF_SCHEDULE_MS[1]` = 10,000ms (10s), skipping the first tier (5s).

Compare with a brand-new command (no existing entry):

```
newCount = (undefined?.count ?? -1) + 1 = -1 + 1 = 0
```

This correctly gets `BACKOFF_SCHEDULE_MS[0]` = 5,000ms (5s).

## The fix

Reset `count` to `-1` instead of `0` when output is received. The next no-output poll then computes `(-1) + 1 = 0`, correctly landing on the first backoff tier.

## Impact

After this fix, commands that produce output then go quiet will poll at 5s -> 10s -> 30s -> 60s instead of 10s -> 30s -> 60s. This improves responsiveness for background commands.

target.title: fix(compaction): preserve partial summary on mid-chain chunk failure
target.body:
## Problem

`summarizeChunks` in `src/agents/compaction.ts` processes message chunks sequentially, building a progressive summary. If chunk N succeeds but chunk N+1 fails after exhausting 3 retries, the exception propagates up and the partial summary from chunks 0..N is lost.

The caller `summarizeWithFallback` catches the error and falls back, but it doesn't have access to the partial summary. This means a transient provider failure on the last chunk discards all summarization work from previous chunks.

## Fix

Track `lastSuccessful` inside the loop. On non-abort/non-timeout failure after retry exhaustion, return the partial summary instead of propagating the error. Abort and timeout errors still propagate immediately since those indicate intentional cancellation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: When a mid-chain chunk fails during compaction summarization, the entire summary was lost (function threw). Now it returns the last successful partial summary.
- Real environment tested: macOS 15, Node 24.15.0, vitest 4.1.6.
- How the proof works: Regression tests exercise the real `summarizeChunks` logic via vitest module mocking. `generateSummary` and `retryAsync` are mocked to control chunk outcomes, but the try/catch preservation logic runs for real.
- Tests added and passing (46 total across 2 shards, 6 new):
  ```
  ok returns partial summary when a later chunk fails with a non-abort error
  ok re-throws abort errors instead of returning partial summary
  ok re-throws timeout errors instead of returning partial summary
  ok returns the full final summary when all chunks succeed
  ok falls back to default when the first chunk fails (no partial to recover)
  ok preserves the latest successful summary in a 3+ chunk chain
  ```
- Key test: Mocks `generateSummary` to succeed for chunk 1 then fail with "API quota exceeded" for chunk 2. Verifies the function returns "Summary of chunk 1" (partial summary) and `log.warn` is called with "chunk summarization failed after retries; returning partial summary".
- Full test run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/compaction.test.ts` -- 46 passed, 0 failed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/compaction.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (GREEN)**:
Terminal output from `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/compaction.test.ts`:
  ```
  ✓ |agents-core| ../../src/agents/compaction.test.ts (23 tests) 4639ms
  ✓ |agents-support| ../../src/agents/compaction.test.ts (23 tests) 1217ms
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
       Tests  46 passed (46)
  ```
- **Red-green verified**: Reverting the `lastSuccessful` tracking causes exactly the 2 partial-recovery tests to fail (×4 across shards), confirming they exercise the fix:
  ```
  × returns partial summary when a later chunk fails with a non-abort error
    Expected: "Summary of chunk 1"
    Received: "Context contained 2 messages (0 oversized). Summary unavailable due to size limits."
  × preserves the latest successful summary in a 3+ chunk chain
    Expected: "Summary after chunks 1+2"
    Received: "Context contained 3 messages (0 oversized). Summary unavailable due to size limits."
  Test Files  2 failed (2)
       Tests  4 failed | 42 passed (46)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: After running `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`, when chunk 1 succeeds but chunk 2 fails with `"API quota exceeded"`, the function returns `"Summary of chunk 1"` (partial summary) and `log.warn` fires with `"chunk summarization failed after retries; returning partial summary"`. Before the fix, the exception propagated and the partial summary was lost entirely.
- What was not tested: Real LLM API failure during compaction with a live model.

target.title: feat(context-pruning): cache media to disk during pruning instead of stripping
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Context pruning removes image/media blocks and replaces them with `[image removed during context pruning]`, losing media permanently from the session
- Why it matters: Agents lose access to previously-shared images mid-conversation, degrading multi-turn visual reasoning
- What changed: When `cacheMedia: true` is configured, pruned media is saved to disk (`~/.openclaw/media/cache/`) and replaced with `[media cached: <path>]` markers that agents can read back on demand
- What did NOT change: Default behavior (`cacheMedia: false`) is unchanged — existing pruning logic preserved

### Bug fixes included (post-review)

| # | File | Bug | Fix |
|---|------|-----|-----|
| 1 | `pruner.ts` | soft-trim + hard-clear: `PRUNED_CONTEXT_IMAGE_MARKER` text was discarded when a message went through both phases, breaking `writePrunedMediaCaches` | Replaced `softTrimmedIndexes` Set with `softPhaseMediaCount` Map; hard-clear re-injects the right number of placeholder markers |
| 2 | `extension.ts` | multi-image hard-clear: `writePrunedMediaCaches` restarted the full `refs` loop for every content block, so block N got ref 0's cached path instead of ref N's | Changed to a single `refIdx` counter advancing sequentially across all blocks |
| 3 | `media-cache.ts` | TOCTOU race: `access()` then `writeFile()` with concurrent `Promise.allSettled` writes — a racing caller could observe a partially-written file | Write to `${hash}.pid.tmp` then `rename()` atomically; concurrent writers landing first are handled gracefully |
| 4 | `media-cache.ts` | MIME normalization: `mediaCacheKind` and `buildCachedMediaMarker` passed raw MIME to `mediaKindFromMime`, so `image/png; charset=binary` or `IMAGE/PNG` → `kind=document` → `images.ts` silently dropped the cached image | Both functions now call `normalizeMimeType()` before classification/embedding |
| 5 | `pruner.ts` | `extractCachedMediaMarkers` preserved entire text blocks (including soft-trim head/tail and trim notes) into hard-cleared output, defeating the clear | Now extracts only the `[media cached: ...]` marker strings via regex, not the surrounding block content |

- [x] Feature
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue/PR

- Supersedes #53869 (closed for consolidation)

N/A — new feature with post-review bug fixes

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

1. Set `agents.defaults.contextPruning.cacheMedia: true`
2. Send images to agent in a long conversation
3. When context pruning triggers, verify images cached to `~/.openclaw/media/cache/`
4. Verify `[media cached: ...]` markers appear in context

## Evidence

target.title: feat(exec): expose knownLongFlags in safeBinProfiles config schema
target.body:
## Summary

Expose the `knownLongFlags` field in the `safeBinProfiles` config schema, allowing operators to declare long flags as "known" for GNU abbreviation resolution without misusing `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`.

Closes #56805

## Problem

The `knownLongFlags` field exists internally in `SafeBinProfile` and is used by `validateSafeBinArgv` for GNU long-option abbreviation resolution. However, it is not present in:

1. The Zod schema (`ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema`) — `.strict()` causes an "Unrecognized key" crash
2. The `SafeBinProfileFixture` type — no way to pass it through config normalization

For empty profiles `{}`, `knownLongFlags` auto-derives as `[]`, causing **all** long flags to be treated as unknown and rejected. The only workaround is to list every possible long flag in `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`, which changes their semantics.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags: z.array(z.string()).optional()` to `ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to `SafeBinProfileFixture` type; update `normalizeSafeBinProfileFixture` and `compileSafeBinProfile` to normalize and merge with auto-derived flags |
| `src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to the type cast in normalization |
| `src/config/schema.base.generated.ts` | Regenerated via `generate-base-config-schema.ts` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` | 6 new tests for the config path |

## Design Decisions

- **Merge, don't replace**: User-provided `knownLongFlags` are merged with auto-derived flags from `allowedValueFlags ∪ deniedFlags`, ensuring flags in those lists are always "known" even if not explicitly listed
- **Reuses `normalizeFixtureFlags`**: Same normalization (trim, dedupe, sort) applied to `knownLongFlags` as to other flag arrays
- **Backwards-compatible**: Existing configs without `knownLongFlags` work identically via auto-derivation

## Usage

```json
{
  "tools": {
    "exec": {
      "safeBins": ["mycli"],
      "safeBinProfiles": {
        "mycli": {
          "knownLongFlags": ["--verbose", "--version", "--help"]
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Test Plan

- [x] All 96 tests in `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` pass (90 existing + 6 new)
- [x] All 90 tests across related test files pass (`exec-approvals-safe-bins`, `exec-safe-bin-runtime-policy`, `io.compat`, `doctor/exec-safe-bins`, `doctor-config-flow.safe-bins`, `pi-tools.safe-bins`)
- [x] Generated schema test passes
- [x] Pre-commit hooks pass (oxlint, type checking, conflict markers)

target.title: Fail closed on ambiguous gateway agent errors
target.body:
## Summary
- fail closed for `openclaw agent` when the gateway error indicates the remote request may still be active
- keep embedded fallback for recoverable gateway errors like "gateway not connected"
- add focused tests for timeout/closed fail-closed behavior

## Why
In real ClawTeam/OpenClaw unattended runs, a gateway timeout could be followed by an immediate embedded fallback on the same session. If the gateway-side turn was still alive and holding the session write lock, the embedded retry would contend on the same `.jsonl.lock` and turn a real in-flight run into a misleading session-lock failure.

This change makes the CLI fail closed for ambiguous gateway errors instead of re-entering locally on the same session.

## Validation
- `pnpm -C /Users/zky/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-src test src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts`
- real local CLI check against a bad gateway URL now returns an explicit "without embedded fallback" error instead of falling back to embedded

- Why it matters: Blocks card-based workflows (weekly reports, approvals, form-like actions) because the bot cannot read card content.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

target.title: fix(signal): forward quote metadata to agent context
target.body:
## Summary

Signal quoted replies (using the native reply/quote feature) arrive with `dataMessage.quote` containing `id`, `author`, and `text` from signal-cli, but only `quote.text` was being extracted — `id` and `author` were silently dropped, and none of the `ReplyTo*` context fields were populated in `finalizeInboundContext()`.

This means agents receiving Signal messages cannot tell what message a user was replying to, even though the data is available. The reply text itself arrives fine, but the quote context (which message was being replied to, who wrote it) is lost.

Telegram already handles this correctly via `describeReplyTarget()` which populates `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, and `ReplyToIsQuote`. This PR mirrors that behavior for Signal.

## Changes

- **`event-handler.types.ts`**: Expand `SignalDataMessage.quote` type to include `id`, `author`, `authorNumber` fields (matching signal-cli JSON-RPC protocol)
- **`event-handler.ts`**: 
  - Add `signalQuote` field to `SignalInboundEntry` type
  - Extract quote metadata from `dataMessage.quote` before debouncer enqueue
  - Set `ReplyToId`, `ReplyToBody`, `ReplyToSender`, `ReplyToIsQuote` in inbound context payload

## Before / After

**Before:** Agent receives `Body: "yes that sounds good"` — no indication this was a reply to a specific message.

**After:** Agent receives:
- `Body: "yes that sounds good"` 
- `ReplyToId: "1774743992672"` (timestamp of quoted message)
- `ReplyToBody: "Should we deploy the fix tonight?"` (original message text)
- `ReplyToSender: "+1234567890"` (who wrote the original)
- `ReplyToIsQuote: true`

## Test plan

- [ ] Send a normal (non-reply) Signal message → verify no ReplyTo fields set
- [ ] Send a quoted reply → verify ReplyToId, ReplyToBody, ReplyToSender, ReplyToIsQuote populated
- [ ] Send a reply with no new text (quote-only) → verify bodyText falls back to quote text, ReplyTo fields still set
- [ ] Send a message in a group with a quote → verify same behavior in group context
- [ ] Verify Telegram reply behavior unchanged

target.title: fix(telegram): respect topic-level groupPolicy overrides for /commands in forum topics (fixes #56699)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #56699 — native commands (`/reset`, `/new`, `/status`) in Telegram forum supergroups were being processed by all bots even when a topic had `groupPolicy: \"disabled\"` configured.

**Root cause:** `resolveTelegramCommandAuth` called `evaluateTelegramGroupPolicyAccess` with `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: false`. This caused the policy resolver to skip `topicConfig.groupPolicy` and `groupConfig.groupPolicy` overrides, falling back to the account-level policy. When a topic was configured with `groupPolicy: \"disabled\"`, that override was ignored for commands but respected for regular messages.

**Fix:** Change `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: false` → `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: true` so native commands use the same topic/group-level policy resolution that regular messages use (`bot-handlers.runtime.ts:586`).

## Test plan

- [x] All 7 existing `bot-native-commands.group-auth.test.ts` tests pass
- [x] All 76 Telegram extension test files pass (1113 tests)
- [x] TypeScript type-check passes
- [x] Manual: in a forum supergroup with `topics["N"].groupPolicy: "disabled"`, `/reset` in topic N should respond with "Telegram group commands are disabled." only for bots that have that topic disabled

target.title: fix(proxy): redact sensitive headers in standalone debug proxy captures
target.body:
## Problem

The standalone debug proxy (`openclaw proxy start`) stores raw request and response headers in capture events via `JSON.stringify(req.headers)` without redaction. This includes `Authorization`, `x-api-key`, `Cookie`, and other sensitive headers in the capture database.

The runtime proxy path (`runtime.ts`) already uses `redactedCaptureHeaders()` with a comprehensive sensitive-header allowlist (lines 19-41), but the standalone `proxy-server.ts` does not. This inconsistency means captured traffic from `openclaw proxy start` contains secrets that `openclaw proxy run` would redact.

## Fix

Add a `redactHeaders` function to `proxy-server.ts` that delegates sensitivity
detection to the **shared** `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` function exported from
`runtime.ts`. This eliminates the duplicate header lists and ensures the
standalone proxy and the in-process fetch-patch always use the identical
sensitivity logic (exact names + fragment matching). The function is applied to
all three header storage points:
1. HTTP request headers (outbound capture)
2. HTTP response headers (inbound capture)
3. CONNECT request headers (tunnel capture)

**Centralization**: `proxy-server.ts` imports `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` from
`runtime.ts` (lines 20–41 there) rather than duplicating the constant lists.
Changes to the sensitive-header vocabulary are now single-source.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Debug proxy capture events stored raw `Authorization`, `Cookie`, `X-Api-Key`, and other sensitive headers in the capture database.
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node 26.0.0, vitest 4.1.7.
- How the proof works: Regression tests exercise the real `redactHeaders` function (exported for testing). Tests cover all 11 exact-match sensitive header names, 7 substring fragment patterns, case-insensitive matching, array-valued headers, empty headers, and mixed header maps.
- Tests added and passing (13 total, 10 new):
  ```
  ok redacts all exact-match sensitive header names
  ok preserves non-sensitive headers unchanged
  ok redacts headers matching sensitive fragments
  ok matches header names case-insensitively
  ok handles empty headers object
  ok preserves undefined header values for non-sensitive headers
  ok redacts sensitive headers with array values
  ok handles mixed sensitive and non-sensitive headers together
  ok handles header names with leading/trailing whitespace via trim
  ok does not redact fragment-like values in non-matching header names
  ```
- Full test run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/proxy-capture/proxy-server.test.ts` -- 13 passed, 0 failed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/proxy-capture/proxy-server.test.ts -t "redact"
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
Terminal output from `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/proxy-capture/proxy-server.test.ts`:
  ```
  ✓ redacts all exact-match sensitive header names
  ✓ preserves non-sensitive headers unchanged
  ✓ redacts headers matching sensitive fragments
  ✓ matches header names case-insensitively
  ✓ handles empty headers object
  ✓ redacts sensitive headers with array values
  ✓ handles mixed sensitive and non-sensitive headers together
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  13 passed (13)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: After running `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`, `redactHeaders` replaces values for `authorization`, `cookie`, `x-api-key`, and all 11 exact-match header names with `[REDACTED]`. Fragment matching catches `x-custom-api-key-v2` and `my-secret-header`. Non-sensitive headers like `content-type` pass through unchanged. Before the fix, all headers were stored raw via `JSON.stringify(req.headers)`.
- What was not tested: Live proxy traffic capture with real HTTP requests containing auth headers.

## Blocker fixes (ClawSweeper review)

1. **Compile blocker (duplicate declarations)**: The original PR had
   `SENSITIVE_HEADER_NAMES`, `SENSITIVE_HEADER_FRAGMENTS`, and `redactHeaders`
   declared twice in `proxy-server.ts`. Removed all duplicates; now one clean
   `redactHeaders` function that delegates to the shared import.
2. **Centralize redaction**: `proxy-server.ts` now imports
   `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` from `runtime.ts` instead of maintaining a
   separate copy of the sensitive-header lists. Zero drift risk.
3. **Red-green verified**: 13 tests pass with redaction; without the
   `redactHeaders` call the raw `JSON.stringify(req.headers)` path stores
   secrets in plain text.

target.title: TTS: enforce standalone directive lines parsing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: TTS directives were previously parsed with regex-heavy matching, which made standalone-line requirements implicit and easy to misinterpret (especially for text blocks and mixed line endings).
- Why it matters: Ambiguous parsing can cause unexpected directive activation, inconsistent cleaned text behavior, and regression risk when input formatting varies (e.g., CRLF, extra blank lines, multiple text blocks).
- What changed:
  - Introduced internal line-by-line parsing helpers in `src/tts/directives.ts` with explicit states (`normal` / `in_tts_text_block`).
  - Made standalone directive rules explicit in behavior:
    - `[[tts:...]]` is recognized only when it occupies a full trimmed line.
    - `[[tts:text]]` and `[[/tts:text]]` are recognized only when each appears on its own line.
  - Preserved external API shape (`TtsDirectiveParseResult`) and compatibility of returned fields.
  - Added regression coverage in `src/plugins/contracts/tts.contract.test.ts` for:
    - CRLF (`\r\n`) directive parsing.
    - Multiple consecutive `[[tts:text]]...[[/tts:text]]` blocks (first block wins).
    - Stable blank-line preservation in `cleanedText`.
  - Updated tagged-mode auto-TTS test input to use standalone block form so tests reflect intended directive semantics.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - No change to public parse result type shape.
  - No change to provider override token semantics beyond standalone-line triggering requirements.
  - No changes to unrelated TTS synthesis transport/provider runtime behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #<fill-if-applicable>
- Related #<fill-if-applicable>
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: Directive recognition rules were embedded in broad regexes, so “standalone line only” behavior was implicit rather than explicit in parser control flow.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Contract coverage did not lock in CRLF behavior, multi-block precedence, and blank-line stability around directive stripping.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Existing parser behavior relied on regex replacements for both directive lines and `tts:text` block extraction.
- Why this regressed now: As directive usage expanded, edge cases became more visible (line ending variations and block formatting differences).
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/contracts/tts.contract.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Standalone directive parsing with CRLF.
  - First-block-wins behavior for multiple `tts:text` blocks.
  - Stable whitespace retention in `cleanedText` after directive removal.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The contract suite exercises parser behavior through the same exported seams used by runtime TTS flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing parse directive tests cover baseline extraction and inline literal non-trigger behavior.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A (new tests were added).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Inline literal directive strings (embedded inside normal sentence text) remain non-triggering.
- Directive activation is now strictly tied to standalone lines for `[[tts:...]]` and `[[tts:text]]` block markers.
- Behavior is consistent across LF and CRLF inputs.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[text with directive-like patterns]
  -> regex replace paths
  -> directive activation could be harder to reason about from control flow

After:
[text]
  -> line scanner (normal / in_tts_text_block)
  -> standalone line checks
  -> structured parse result
  -> deterministic cleanedText + overrides
