target.title: Revamp command authorization candidates
target.body:
## Summary

- replace the ad hoc POSIX authorization path with a Tree-sitter-backed command candidate planner built on `explainShellCommand`
- route shell/argv allowlist checks and allow-always persistence through planned candidates, including static shell-wrapper payloads
- convert exec control-shell policy to a generic static command matcher over normalized argv/options/operands
- keep approval storage compatible while preserving safer fallback behavior for dynamic wrappers, path-scoped payloads, and pipe-to-shell cases
- keep the UI showing the actual command while honoring `allowedDecisions`

## Decisions and rationale

- POSIX shell authorization now comes from one planned command model: `planShellAuthorization()` uses the Tree-sitter-backed `explainShellCommand()` path, and the plan is carried into allowlist evaluation, allow-always persistence, and shell rewrite. The old POSIX splitter/render fallback is no longer an alternate source of candidates.
- Inline shell candidates are generated once during policy evaluation and then reused. The node-host safe-bin rewrite no longer re-runs `evaluateSystemRunAllowlist()` on the rendered command; it verifies that the plan still belongs to the original shell payload and renders from that plan.
- Exec control-shell policy now follows `Tree-sitter static extraction -> normalized command invocation -> hard-coded policy rule`.
- `/approve` handling is removed from exec control-shell policy. `/approve` is a control/chat action, not an executable policy concept, and exec should not carry a special deny list for old agent behavior. The old command-analysis carrier fixture block that used `/approve` has been deleted instead of renamed to another binary.
- Safe bins are treated as narrow, argument-profiled execution shortcuts, not blanket shell trust. Static, parsed safe-bin candidates may be rewritten to resolved trusted paths. Dynamic shells, command substitution, line continuations, path-scoped wrapper payloads, and pipe-to-shell cases remain approval-gated unless the whole relevant command graph is allowed.
- Static reads under `.ssh` / `~/.ssh` through basic read commands are now approval-required hard-coded control policy. Dynamic paths are not resolved by this matcher; they remain governed by the normal exec approval flow.
- Background `&` is preserved as shell topology. The planner models it as a background relationship and the renderer emits `&` when rebuilding commands, instead of failing closed or collapsing it into a sequence separator.
- `deny` still wins when deny-mode policy is active. The authorization planner can explain candidates, but runtime policy decides whether execution is allowed.

## Behavior changes

- Static POSIX shell wrappers like `sh -c 'git status'` can now be authorized by the inner `git` candidate when the payload is static and safely parsed.
- Dynamic wrappers like `sh -c '$CMD'`, command substitution, line continuations, and path-scoped wrapper payloads stay approval-gated instead of becoming reusable inner approvals.
- `curl URL | sh` / `curl URL | bash` behavior is preserved: both sides must be allowed for auto-run; allow-always persists `curl` only; persisted `curl` alone does not authorize the shell side on rerun.
- Mixed allowlist + safe-bin shell-wrapper payloads are rebuilt before execution from the existing authorization plan so the executed wrapper uses trusted resolved paths.
- Exec control-shell policy denies OpenClaw channel login commands and requires approval for security audit suppression mutation through the same normalized command matcher.
- Static `cat` / `less` / `more` / `head` / `tail` reads under `.ssh` or `~/.ssh` now require explicit approval.
- `ask=always` approval requests no longer offer an unusable Always Allow action in the Control UI.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: Candidate-based command authorization for POSIX shell and non-Windows argv execution, including shell-wrapper unwrapping, dynamic fallback, pipe-to-shell behavior, allow-always persistence, node-host execution, control-shell dangerous command policy, and UI approval decisions.

Real environment tested: Local macOS focused tests; local OpenClaw Control UI against the candidate worktree; Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-authorization-plan.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-allowlist.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-allow-always.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-analysis.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-policy.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts
git diff --check
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox --blacksmith-org openclaw --blacksmith-workflow .github/workflows/ci-check-testbox.yml --blacksmith-job check --blacksmith-ref main --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json -- CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=6 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 OPENCLAW_TESTBOX=1 OPENCLAW_TESTBOX_REMOTE_RUN=1 pnpm check:changed
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/node-host/invoke-system-run-allowlist.test.ts src/infra/exec-control-shell-policy.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-approvals-allow-always.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-allowlist.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-plan.test.ts src/infra/exec-control-shell-policy.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run-allowlist.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-control-shell-policy.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.script-preflight.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/command-analysis/risks.test.ts src/infra/exec-control-shell-policy.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.script-preflight.test.ts
git diff HEAD --check
```

Evidence after fix: Focused local proof passed 15 files / 465 tests. `git diff --check` passed. Testbox-through-Crabbox `tbx_01ks1f6v0x35t56nymmxbaw4a1` / GitHub Actions run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26135215597 completed `pnpm check:changed` with exit 0. Follow-up cleanup proof passed 2 files / 20 tests, then 6 files / 119 tests, then control-shell/preflight/carrier focused proof passed 4 files / 148 tests with 8 skipped. After removing the repo doc and deleting the old carrier fixture block, focused proof passed 4 files / 147 tests with 8 skipped, and `git diff HEAD --check` passed.

Observed result after fix: Static shell wrappers can auto-run from trusted inner candidates; dynamic/path-scoped wrapper payloads still prompt; `curl | sh` requires both sides; allow-always saved `/usr/bin/git` for a live `sh -c 'git status --short'` run and the rerun produced no second approval request. Static `.ssh` reads through basic read commands now require approval.

What was not tested: Full release validation was not run. Windows behavior remains on the legacy fallback path. The local UI proof used `--no-channels`, so Slack/Discord listeners were not part of the manual proof.

## UI proof notes

- Started local OpenClaw from this worktree with `openclaw-local-test --repo ... --no-open --no-channels --ttl 2h`.
- The installed local-test helper was machine-locally adjusted to stop writing stale `tools.exec.mode = "auto"`; that was not a repo change.
- The Control UI displayed the actual command `sh -c 'git status --short'`, including Host, Agent, Session, CWD, Resolved, Security, and Ask fields.
- Clicking Always Allow resolved the prompt.
- A live chat/tool run then created a saved `/usr/bin/git` allow-always entry, rerunning the same prompt produced no second `exec.approval.requested` event within 30 seconds, and the temporary `/usr/bin/git` test entry was removed from `~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json` afterward.

target.title: feat(openresponses): return reasoning/thinking content in /v1/responses output
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The `/v1/responses` endpoint does not return model reasoning/thinking content. When models produce chain-of-thought reasoning (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI o-series, Gemini thinking, Ollama thinking models), the reasoning output is silently discarded by the HTTP API. Callers have no way to observe model reasoning through the OpenResponses interface.
- **Why it matters:** The [Open Responses spec](https://www.openresponses.org/reference) defines `type: "reasoning"` as a first-class output item (`ReasoningBody`) and `response.reasoning.delta`/`response.reasoning.done` as streaming events. Not implementing these means OpenClaw's OpenResponses endpoint is incomplete relative to the spec, and API callers (dashboards, orchestrators, observability tools) cannot access reasoning data.
- **What changed:** The endpoint now captures reasoning content from agent thinking events and returns it as `type: "reasoning"` output items (non-stream) or `response.reasoning.delta`/`response.reasoning.done` SSE events (stream). When no assistant text is produced but reasoning exists, the reasoning text is used as a fallback response to avoid empty replies from thinking-only models.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No config changes required. No new dependencies. Existing behavior for non-reasoning models is unchanged. The `onReasoningStream` callback path for channel-specific reasoning delivery (Discord, Telegram, etc.) is unmodified. This change only adds reasoning to the HTTP API surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #54496
- Related #44513
- Related #8364
- Partially addresses #31449 (OpenResponses reasoning output/events only; full tool I/O/context parity still pending)
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature implementing an existing spec item.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

### Non-streaming responses

When reasoning is produced, the response `output` array now includes a `type: "reasoning"` item before the assistant message:

```json
{
  "output": [
    {
      "type": "reasoning",
      "id": "reasoning_abc123",
      "content": "Let me think about this step by step..."
    },
    {
      "type": "message",
      "role": "assistant",
      "content": [{ "type": "output_text", "text": "The answer is 42." }]
    }
  ]
}
```

### Streaming responses

Two new SSE event types are emitted during streaming:

- `response.reasoning.delta` — cumulative reasoning text as it streams
- `response.reasoning.done` — final reasoning text when complete

### Fallback behavior

When a model produces reasoning but no assistant text (common with thinking-only model configurations), the reasoning content is used as the response text instead of returning "No response from OpenClaw."

## Diagram (if applicable)

```
Model produces reasoning + answer:

  [thinking events] ──→ collect reasoning ──→ output[0]: reasoning item
  [assistant events] ──→ collect text     ──→ output[1]: assistant message

Model produces reasoning only (no assistant text):

  [thinking events] ──→ collect reasoning ──→ output[0]: reasoning item
  (no assistant text)                     ──→ output[1]: assistant = reasoning fallback
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No — reasoning content was already produced by the model, it was just being discarded

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.4 (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime: Node 22, Bun 1.2
- Model: any reasoning-capable model (Claude, o-series, Gemini thinking)

### Steps

1. Send a POST to `/v1/responses` with a reasoning-capable model
2. Observe the response `output` array

### Expected

Reasoning content appears as `type: "reasoning"` output item.

### Actual

Same as expected.

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm build` green
- [x] `pnpm check` green (lint, format, types)
- [x] `src/gateway/openresponses-http.test.ts` — 12/12 tests pass
- [x] `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.*` — 21/30 pass (9 pre-existing upstream failures, verified same count on clean `upstream/main`)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified: non-stream reasoning collection, stream reasoning events, fallback from thinking to text, cleanup on error
- Edge cases: no reasoning produced (no-op), reasoning-only response (fallback), tool-call + reasoning combined output
- Not verified: production load, all provider-specific thinking formats

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — adds new output items, does not remove or change existing ones
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: reasoning content could be large (10k+ chars) for complex prompts, increasing response payload size
  - Mitigation: This mirrors the model's actual output; callers can ignore the reasoning item if not needed
- Risk: double `emitAgentEvent` calls (raw + formatted) for channel-streaming reasoning mode
  - Mitigation: The raw-only event fires first for HTTP capture; the formatted event fires only when `streamReasoning` is active. HTTP consumers filter by `rawText` presence.


Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

target.title: feat: fire session_end hook on subagent completion and cron session end
target.body:
## Summary

Extends the `session_end` plugin hook to fire in two additional lifecycle cases that were previously missing.

### Problem

The `session_end` hook only fired on session reset/replace (one narrow path in `session.ts`). This made it impossible for plugins to react to task completion without polling — blocking use cases like dispatch queues, memory consolidation, and post-session cleanup.

Tracked in:
- Fixes #10142 (session:end event for workflow integration)
- Related to #31266 (session-memory hook wanting session:end)

### Changes

**`src/agents/subagent-spawn.ts`**
- Fires `session_end` alongside the existing `subagent_ended` hook when a subagent completes
- Guarded with `hookRunner?.hasHooks('session_end')` — zero overhead if no listeners
- Uses `buildSessionEndHookPayload` for consistent payload shape

**`src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`**
- Fires `session_end` after the cron isolated agent session completes and session entry is persisted
- Same guard pattern

**`src/plugins/types.ts`**
- Updated `session_end` comment to document all three trigger cases: session reset/replace, subagent completion, cron completion

**`CHANGELOG.md`**
- Added entry under Unreleased

### Backward Compatibility

- `subagent_ended` hook continues to fire alongside `session_end` — no breaking change
- All new calls are opt-in via the existing `hasHooks` guard

### Motivation

This unblocks the [openclaw-dispatch](https://clawhub.com) plugin (currently in development) which needs to know when sessions complete to drain a task queue without heartbeat polling.

target.title: feat(cron): add agent-turn mode for failure alerts
target.body:
Closes #56521

## What

Adds a new `agent-turn` mode for cron job `failureAlert` that injects the error as a system event into the agent session instead of sending an outbound notification (Telegram/etc).

## Why

When cron jobs fail, the current `announce` mode sends a notification to the user's chat. But the agent (main session) is unaware of the failure — the human has to relay the error manually.

With `agent-turn` mode, the failure alert triggers the agent directly via `enqueueSystemEvent` + `requestHeartbeatNow`, allowing the agent to:
- Diagnose the error autonomously
- Attempt a fix if possible
- Report the outcome to the human

## How

- New `failureAlert.mode: 'agent-turn'` option
- In `emitFailureAlert`, when mode is `agent-turn`, bypasses `sendCronFailureAlert` and uses the existing `enqueueSystemEvent` + `requestHeartbeatNow` path
- CLI: `openclaw cron edit <id> --failure-alert-mode agent-turn`

## Usage

```bash
openclaw cron edit <job-id> --failure-alert-mode agent-turn
```

Or in config:
```json
{
  "failureAlert": {
    "mode": "agent-turn",
    "after": 1
  }
}
```

target.title: fix(agents): log warnings instead of swallowing subagent errors
target.body:
## Problem

Two error-swallowing catch blocks in the subagent lifecycle hide failures that leave subagents orphaned:

1. **`emitSubagentEndedHookOnce`** in `subagent-registry-completion.ts` (line 116): empty `catch {}` silently returns `false` when the hook runner fails. The caller never learns why the ended hook did not fire.

2. **`restoreSubagentRunsOnce`** in `subagent-registry.ts` (line 625): bare `catch {}` with only a comment "ignore restore failures". If disk restore, orphan reconciliation, or sweeper startup fails, subagents stay orphaned with no diagnostic trail.

## Fix

Replace both empty catch blocks with `catch (err) { log.warn("...", { err }); }`, matching the existing pattern used by `notifyContextEngineSubagentEnded` at line 410 in the same file.

Added `createSubsystemLogger` to `subagent-registry-completion.ts` since it had no logger.

## Real behavior proof — red-green verified

### Test strategy

Both changed call sites are tested by mocking the logger module (`../logging/subsystem.js`) to capture `log.warn` calls, then forcing the code paths to throw by making internal dependencies reject. Each test asserts `log.warn` is called with the expected message and error object.

### Call site 1: `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` (subagent-registry-completion.ts)

**Test**: "logs a warning when hook execution throws instead of swallowing silently"

- Mocks `runSubagentEnded` to reject with `new Error("hook runner crashed")`
- Calls `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce()`
- Asserts: `log.warn("emitSubagentEndedHookOnce failed", { err: hookError })` called exactly once

### Call site 2: `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` (subagent-registry.ts)

**Test**: "logs a warning when restoring subagent runs from disk throws instead of swallowing silently"

- Mocks `restoreSubagentRunsFromDisk` to throw `new Error("corrupt state file")`
- Calls `initSubagentRegistry()` (which calls `restoreSubagentRunsOnce`)
- Asserts: `log.warn("subagent restore from disk failed", { err: restoreError })` called exactly once

### GREEN with fix (all pass)

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (9 tests)
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (9 tests)
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  18 passed (18)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry.test.ts (30 tests)
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry.test.ts (30 tests)
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  60 passed (60)
```

### RED without fix (new tests fail)

Reverted both catch blocks to silent `catch {}`, kept tests:

```
 FAIL  |agents-core| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts > emitSubagentEndedHookOnce > logs a warning when hook execution throws instead of swallowing silently
AssertionError: expected "vi.fn()" to be called 1 times, but got 0 times

 FAIL  |agents-support| subagent-registry.test.ts > subagent registry seam flow > logs a warning when restoring subagent runs from disk throws instead of swallowing silently
AssertionError: expected "vi.fn()" to be called with ..., but got 0 times

 Tests  4 failed (completion: 2, registry: 2)
```

- What was not tested: Live subagent hook failure or disk restore failure in a running OpenClaw instance.

target.title: fix(security): escape entry.id in HTML export to prevent attribute XSS
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `entry.id` from deserialized trajectory JSON is interpolated directly into HTML `id` and `data-entry-id` attributes without escaping in the HTML export template.
- Why it matters: A malicious plugin or tampered trajectory can inject event handlers via crafted `entry.id`, executing arbitrary JavaScript when the exported HTML is opened by another user.
- What changed: Wrapped `entry.id` with `escapeHtmlAttr()` at both HTML insertion points — the `id` attribute in `renderEntry()` and the `data-entry-id` attribute in `renderCopyLinkButton()`.
- Copy-link regression fixed: The original PR double-escaped `entry.id` (escaped before passing to `renderCopyLinkButton`, then escaped again inside it). This caused `dataset.entryId` to return the HTML-encoded value instead of the raw ID, breaking `buildShareUrl()`. Fix: escape only at the HTML insertion boundary — raw `entry.id` is passed to `renderCopyLinkButton()`, which handles its own `escapeHtmlAttr()` call internally. Browsers decode HTML entities in `dataset` reads, so the round-trip is safe: `escapeHtmlAttr(id)` → HTML attr → `dataset.entryId` → raw `id`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new helpers added; uses the existing `escapeHtmlAttr()` already used at 19+ other call sites in the same file.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: The file already uses `escapeHtml()` and `escapeHtmlAttr()` for all other user-facing content (19+ call sites), making this an inconsistency.
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `entry.id` was interpolated raw into `data-entry-id` HTML attribute, allowing attribute-injection XSS.
- Real environment tested: macOS 15, Node 24.15.0, vitest 4.1.6.
- How the proof works: Two regression tests using linkedom (JSDOM-compatible):
  1. **XSS prevention**: Renders with `entry.id = '"><script>alert(1)</script><div data-x="'` and asserts no `<script>` tags are injected, no attribute breakout (`[onmouseover]`, `[data-x]`).
  2. **Copy-link round-trip**: Renders with `entry.id = 'msg-with"quotes&amp\'s'` and asserts `dataset.entryId` returns the raw ID (not the HTML-escaped version), proving `buildShareUrl()` gets the correct value.
- Tests added and passing (10 total, 2 new):
  ```
  ✓ escapes entry.id in element id and data-entry-id attributes
  ✓ copy-link round-trip: dataset.entryId matches raw entry.id after browser decoding
  ```
- Red-green verified:
  - **RED** (without fix): Both new tests fail — `<script>` tags inject into DOM, `dataset.entryId` truncates at unescaped `"`.
  - **GREEN** (with fix): All 10 tests pass.
- Full test run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts` — 10 passed, 0 failed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  ✓ |auto-reply| template.security.test.ts (10 tests) 656ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  10 passed (10)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: `escapeHtmlAttr()` encodes `"`, `'`, `<`, `>`, `&` in attribute values. Linkedom parsing confirms no attribute breakout and `dataset.entryId` returns the raw `entry.id` (browsers decode HTML entities in dataset reads). The copy-link `buildShareUrl()` receives the correct raw ID.
- What was not tested: Browser rendering of the exported HTML file (linkedom covers the parsing behavior).

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `entry.id` was assumed to always be a safe UUID, so it was interpolated raw. All other user content in the file goes through `escapeHtml()` or `escapeHtmlAttr()`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No lint rule or test enforced that all template interpolations into HTML attributes use escaping.
- Contributing context (if known): The `escapeHtmlAttr()` helper already existed at line 668 and was used for all other user-facing content.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: (1) Export with `entry.id` containing `<script>` XSS payload produces no injected script tags. (2) Copy-link `dataset.entryId` round-trips the raw `entry.id` through HTML escaping and browser decoding.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The security test file already tests other escaping scenarios; two more cases cover this attribute injection path and the copy-link regression.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): The 8 existing security tests cover body content escaping, not attribute interpolation of `entry.id`.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. The `entry.id` values in normal use are UUIDs, which contain no characters that need escaping. The fix only affects crafted/malicious IDs.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 (WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node 26.0.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Craft trajectory JSON with `entry.id` set to `a" onclick="alert(document.cookie)"`
2. Run the HTML export template against this trajectory
3. Inspect the generated HTML for attribute breakout

### Expected

- The `id` and `data-entry-id` attributes contain entity-encoded quotes

### Actual

- After fix: Quotes are encoded as `"`, no attribute breakout

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (red-green verified)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

All 10 security tests pass (`template.security.test.ts`), including 2 new tests for this fix.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Malicious `entry.id` with `<script>` injection, attribute breakout payloads, and special chars (`"`, `'`, `&`). All are properly escaped by `escapeHtmlAttr()`.
- Edge cases checked: Copy-link round-trip with `dataset.entryId` returning raw value, `getElementById` matching the escaped-then-decoded DOM `id`, normal UUID `entry.id` (unchanged output).
- What you did **not** verify: Full end-to-end export flow with a running OpenClaw instance. The template function was tested in isolation with linkedom.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

None. The fix uses an existing helper that is already applied to 19+ other interpolation sites in the same file.

target.title: feat: Add opt-in ACP parent completion notify for sessions_spawn
target.body:
## What changed

This adds an opt-in ACP parent update mode for `sessions_spawn` so ACP `mode:"run"` completions can return to the parent session like subagent completions, instead of only surfacing as parent-side system events.

Key behavior changes:

- adds `parentUpdates?: "system" | "notify"` for ACP spawns
- keeps the default behavior unchanged
- allows `parentUpdates:"notify"` for ACP `mode:"run"` even without `streamTo:"parent"`
- keeps `streamTo:"parent"` as the progress relay switch
- routes terminal completion/error through the existing subagent-style announce flow when `parentUpdates:"notify"` is enabled
- falls back to the existing system-event path if notify/announce is unavailable

## Why

Today, ACP `sessions_spawn` run completions only surface to the parent session as system events.

That lets the parent session observe that work finished internally, but the completion signal primarily rides the heartbeat/internal relay path instead of a normal completion delivery path. In practice, channel-facing completion notifications can be missing or incomplete, and final result details are often lost.

This change fixes that gap by adding an explicit, non-default completion routing mode for ACP run spawns.

## Design

This intentionally splits progress relay from terminal completion routing:

- `streamTo:"parent"` continues to control progress relay
- `parentUpdates:"notify"` controls terminal completion announce

That split keeps progress on the lightweight best-effort relay path while reusing the existing announce/completion machinery for final outcomes.

## Compatibility

- default behavior remains unchanged
- ACP / ACPX execution flow is unchanged
- only parent reporting / relay / completion routing is updated

## Validation

Passed:

- `corepack pnpm test -- src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 ./node_modules/.bin/tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit --pretty false`

Note:

- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts` still has unrelated existing local failures:
  - `sessions_list filters kinds and includes messages` timeout
  - `sessions_send supports fire-and-forget and wait` expects `historyOnlyCalls` length 8 but gets 9

target.title: fix(delivery): log failDelivery errors instead of silently swallowing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Two `failDelivery()` call sites in the outbound delivery pipeline use `.catch(() => {})`, silently swallowing errors when recording a delivery as failed in the queue.
- Why it matters: If `failDelivery` rejects (e.g., database connection lost), the delivery queue entry is left in an inconsistent state with no diagnostic trace. The adjacent `ackDelivery()` handler at lines 1308-1316 properly logs warnings on failure, making this an inconsistency in error handling.
- What changed: Replaced both empty `.catch(() => {})` handlers with `.catch((err) => { log.warn(...) })` that includes the queue ID and formatted error message, matching the existing `ackDelivery` pattern. Added two regression tests covering both rejection paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to delivery logic, queue management, retry behavior, or the `ackDelivery` handler. Only error reporting in the two `failDelivery` catch blocks.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix

- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Skills / tool execution

- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83113
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `failDelivery()` errors silently swallowed by `.catch(() => {})`, leaving no diagnostic trace when queue state updates fail.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux (Grok Build), Node, openclaw main at 1b1580cbc3 with fix cherry-picked.
- **Exact steps or command run**:

- **Evidence after fix (GREEN — 85/85 pass)**:

- **Evidence without fix (RED — 2 new tests fail, 83 pre-existing pass)**:

     × logs a warning when failDelivery rejects on bestEffort partial failure (#83113) 9ms

- **What was not tested**: Live database connection failure during delivery. The proof exercises the real `deliver.ts` code path with injected queue/logger dependencies, not a production instance. Red-green verification confirms the `.catch()` handlers deterministically fire.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The `.catch(() => {})` pattern was used as a fire-and-forget safety net, but omitted any logging. The adjacent `ackDelivery()` call at lines 1308-1316 already follows the correct pattern with `log.warn()`, making this an oversight rather than an intentional design choice.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No lint rule catches empty catch handlers with intentionally discarded errors.
- Contributing context (if known): The two `failDelivery` calls were likely added at different times; the pattern was copied from the (correct) `ackDelivery` call but the logging was omitted.

- Scenario the tests lock in: When `failDelivery` rejects, `log.warn` is called with the queue ID and underlying error message. Two tests added, one per call site:
  1. `logs a warning when failDelivery rejects on bestEffort partial failure (#83113)` — covers the partial-failure path at line ~1298.
  2. `logs a warning when failDelivery rejects in the error handler (#83113)` — covers the outer error-handler path at line ~1334.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Each test mocks `failDelivery` to reject and asserts the warning log contains the queue ID and error message. No other assertions or side effects.
- Red-green verified: Tests fail deterministically without the production fix (`.catch(() => {})` never calls `log.warn`), pass with it.

None. This only adds warning-level log output when an internal queue state update fails, which was previously silent.

target.title: fix: bind Claude permission replies to session
target.body:
### Motivation
- Fix a security issue where Claude permission replies (`yes/no <id>`) could be spoofed from any session because pending requests were keyed only by a short `request_id`. 
- Ensure permission replies are only accepted from the same session that originated the permission request to prevent cross-session or untrusted-channel approvals. 
- Preserve existing behavior for legitimate requests while closing the approval spoofing vector. 

### Description
- Require `session_key` in the `notifications/claude/channel/permission_request` schema and propagate it through the MCP server; changed `src/mcp/channel-shared.ts` and `src/mcp/channel-server.ts`. 
- Store pending Claude permission requests with the originating `sessionKey` and only accept `yes/no <request_id>` replies when the inbound message's `sessionKey` matches; changed `src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts`. 
- Add and update tests to cover session-bound permission handling and regression scenarios, including rejecting attacker-session replies and accepting trusted-session replies; changed `src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts`. 

### Testing
- Ran the targeted unit tests with `pnpm test -- src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts`, which passed (`1 passed, 6 tests`). 
- Ran the repository verification gate with `pnpm check`, which completed successfully (lint/type checks passed).

target.title: fix(gateway): keep two-phase exec approvals pending when no approval …
target.body:
fix(gateway): keep two-phase exec approvals pending when no approval clients connected

## Summary

- Problem: Two-phase exec approval requests (used by agent tools) were immediately expired with `no-approval-route` when no approval clients were connected, same as single-phase (CLI) requests.
- Why it matters: Agent tool calls need to stay pending so that Control UI or webchat can connect with `operator.approvals` scope and resolve the approval before timeout. Expiring them immediately breaks the two-phase approval flow.
- What changed: Added a `!twoPhase` guard to the fast-fail condition in `exec-approval.ts` so only single-phase callers fail fast. Two-phase callers now remain pending until timeout.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Single-phase (CLI) callers still fail fast when no approver is available. No changes to approval forwarding, timeout logic, or the approval manager.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: The fast-fail condition `!hasExecApprovalClients && !forwarded` did not distinguish between single-phase and two-phase callers, expiring both immediately when no approval clients were connected.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test existed that verified two-phase approvals are kept pending when no approval clients are connected.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): The fast-fail path was originally written for CLI (single-phase) only; two-phase support was added later without updating this condition.
- Why this regressed now: Two-phase approval flow began being exercised in environments where approval clients connect asynchronously (Control UI, webchat).
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Two-phase approval requests must NOT be expired when no approver clients are connected; they must remain pending and receive an `accepted` response.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The unit test directly exercises the branch condition with fake timers and verifies that `expire` is not called immediately.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): The prior test only covered the single-phase fast-fail path.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are included in this patch.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Agent tool approval requests now stay pending (instead of immediately failing) when no approval client is connected, giving operators time to connect via Control UI or webchat.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[agent tool call] -> requestExecApproval(twoPhase=true) -> no clients -> expire("no-approval-route") -> DENIED

After:
[agent tool call] -> requestExecApproval(twoPhase=true) -> no clients -> respond("accepted") -> wait for timeout or approval
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux / macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway exec approval
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Start gateway with no approval clients connected.
2. Trigger an agent tool call that sends a two-phase exec approval request.
3. Observe the approval request status.

### Expected

- Two-phase approval stays pending with `accepted` status until timeout or a client connects and resolves it.

### Actual

- Two-phase approval was immediately expired with `no-approval-route`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Two-phase approval stays pending; single-phase still fast-fails.
- Edge cases checked: Timeout expiry still fires for two-phase after `timeoutMs`.
- What you did **not** verify: End-to-end with live Control UI / webchat.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Two-phase requests now wait the full timeout before expiring if no client ever connects.
  - Mitigation: This is the intended behavior; the timeout is already set by the caller and acts as the natural upper bound.

target.title: fix(control-ui): restore header logo and favicon display
target.body:
## Summary

After upgrading to 2026.3.24, the Control UI header logo (red lobster icon) and favicon are missing. The favicon.svg file exists and is accessible at `/favicon.svg`, but the UI doesn't display it.

## Root Cause

`agentLogoUrl()` returned a relative path `favicon.svg` (without leading slash) when `basePath` was empty, which broke resolution in the browser. Additionally, `renderAvatar()` skipped logo rendering entirely when `basePath` was falsy.

## Changes

- `agentLogoUrl()`: Always return `/favicon.svg` (absolute path) when basePath is empty, instead of relative `favicon.svg`
- `renderAvatar()`: Render the logo for assistant role regardless of basePath value
- Updated tests to match the new behavior

## Files Changed

- `ui/src/ui/views/agents-utils.ts` — fixed path generation
- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts` — removed basePath guard
- `ui/src/ui/views/agents-utils.test.ts` — updated test expectations
- `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` — updated test expectations

Fixes #55600

target.title: feat(plugins): add embedding provider contract
target.body:
## Summary

Embeddings are not only a memory feature.
This adds the first plugin-layer contract for general embedding providers, without moving memory or adding a real provider yet.
It gives future provider plugins a `contracts.embeddingProviders` manifest key and `api.registerEmbeddingProvider(...)` registration API so later PRs can bridge memory and add OpenAI-compatible embeddings cleanly.

## What Changed

This is the contract plumbing PR only.
It deliberately avoids adding production provider behavior so the new public surface can be reviewed on its own.

- Added a generic embedding provider registry and runtime export surface.
- Added `contracts.embeddingProviders` manifest parsing and merge support.
- Added `api.registerEmbeddingProvider(...)` to the plugin API and test API.
- Added registry ownership and duplicate-id diagnostics.
- Added plugin record, status, loader snapshot, capability runtime, and bundled capability metadata plumbing.
- Added the public SDK subpath `openclaw/plugin-sdk/embedding-providers` for safe read helpers and types; registration stays behind `api.registerEmbeddingProvider(...)` so manifest ownership is enforced.
- Added focused registry, runtime-resolution, plugin-contract, public-SDK-bypass, and loader rollback tests.
- Updated plugin manifest, SDK overview, SDK subpath, provider plugin, architecture, and capability docs.

## Testing

Latest local checks at `fad85638a03657f8354bb19768c109d5270a8354`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/embedding-provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/loader.test.ts src/plugins/loader.runtime-registry.test.ts src/plugins/embedding-providers.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/embedding-provider.contract.test.ts src/plugins/loader-records.test.ts` -> passed, 6 files / 172 tests.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-embedding-providers.tsbuildinfo` -> passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-embedding-providers.tsbuildinfo` -> passed.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/plugins/embedding-provider-runtime.ts src/plugins/embedding-provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/registry.ts src/plugins/loader.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/embedding-providers.ts src/plugins/contracts/embedding-provider.contract.test.ts` -> passed.
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` -> passed.
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:check-exports` -> passed.
- `node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs` -> passed.
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/plugins/sdk-subpaths.md` -> passed.
- `git diff --check` -> passed.

Earlier docs checks on this branch:

- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check` -> passed.
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/plugins/manifest.md docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md docs/plugins/sdk-subpaths.md docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md docs/plugins/architecture.md docs/plugins/adding-capabilities.md` -> passed.
- `node scripts/check-changed.mjs --help` accidentally ran the fail-safe changed check before later follow-up commits; it completed all selected lanes successfully, including all-project typecheck, lint shards, and runtime import cycle check.

## Risks

The main risk is public SDK surface shape.
This PR adds the registry and API but does not yet migrate memory or any real provider, so runtime behavior should remain unchanged unless a plugin opts into the new API.

## Follow-ups

- Add a memory bridge from general `embeddingProviders` to memory embedding providers.
- Implement `openai-compatible` as a general embedding provider after the bridge exists.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Adds a general plugin contract and registration API for embedding providers; no production provider behavior is enabled in this PR.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout using a temporary real plugin load path and temporary `openclaw.json` config.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `node --import tsx` proof script that created a temporary plugin with `openclaw.plugin.json` declaring `contracts.embeddingProviders: ["proof-embedding"]`, loaded that plugin through OpenClaw's plugin registry/status code, and runtime-registered `api.registerEmbeddingProvider(...)`.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the proof run:

```json
{
  "configPath": "<temp>/state/openclaw.json",
  "pluginLoadPath": "<temp>/proof-embedding",
  "snapshot": {
    "id": "proof-embedding",
    "status": "loaded",
    "embeddingProviderIds": [
      "proof-embedding"
    ]
  },
  "runtime": {
    "id": "proof-embedding",
    "status": "loaded",
    "embeddingProviderIds": [
      "proof-embedding"
    ]
  },
  "inspect": {
    "shape": "plain-capability",
    "capabilities": [
      {
        "kind": "embedding",
        "ids": [
          "proof-embedding"
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
  "registered": {
    "id": "proof-embedding",
    "ownerPluginId": "proof-embedding",
    "defaultModel": "proof-embedding-v1",
    "transport": "local"
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw discovers the manifest-declared `embeddingProviders` contract, reports the plugin as a plain `embedding` capability, runtime-registers the adapter, and preserves the owner plugin id on the registered adapter. Focused follow-up regression tests at `fad85638a03657f8354bb19768c109d5270a8354` also prove cold manifest resolution through the SDK-backed runtime helper, scoped non-activating loads with active global providers, and that the public SDK subpath cannot bypass manifest-owned registration.

What was not tested: Full GitHub CI is running now that the PR is ready for review.

target.title: fix(update): make completion-cache spawn timeout configurable
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS` to override the 30-second `spawnSync` timeout used by `tryWriteCompletionCache` (post-update auto-regen) and `generateCompletionCache` (manual `openclaw doctor completion`). Default behavior unchanged.
- Extracts the env-var read into `resolveCompletionCacheWriteTimeoutMs()` in `src/cli/completion-runtime.ts` so both spawn sites share the same lookup. Removes two duplicated `30_000` constants.
- Adds eight unit tests covering unset / empty / whitespace / non-numeric / zero / negative / positive / whitespace-trimmed env-var values.
- CHANGELOG entry under `2026.5.2 > Fixes`.

## Motivation

Defensive engineering around the post-update completion-cache auto-regen path. 30 seconds is a comfortable budget on a development laptop, but on Pi-class hardware, throttled containers, and low-end VPSes it can be tight: any slowdown in the spawned `openclaw completion --write-state` surfaces as `Completion cache update failed: timed out after 30s. Shell tab-completion may be stale; refresh manually with: openclaw completion --write-state`.

The acute version of this problem — three full plugin sweeps in one `--write-state` invocation, ~61s total on Pi 5 — is fixed by `3b3def03543b` (`fix(plugins): cache cli registration entries`, landed via #75955). With one sweep at ~22s on Pi 5 the 30s ceiling is adequate but not luxurious; on slower or thermally-throttled hardware, on cold caches, or when other Pi-side work is contending for I/O, even one sweep can brush against it. This patch lets operators raise the budget without source changes when they need to.

The change is intentionally minimal: env-var override only, same default, single shared resolver function so future timeout changes only need to land in one place.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/cli/completion-runtime.test.ts` — eight cases, all passing locally on Pi 5 (Duration 405ms, 8/8 passed).
- [ ] Project-wide `pnpm check && pnpm test` via CI.

## Notes for review

- `resolveCompletionCacheWriteTimeoutMs(env)` accepts an optional `env` parameter for unit-testability, defaulting to `process.env`. Matches the pattern of `resolveShellFromEnv(env)` in the same file.
- Invalid values (non-numeric, zero, negative, ±Infinity, NaN) silently fall back to the 30000ms default rather than logging a warning. Felt right for a defensive timeout knob — the alternative would be log spam at every spawn site if someone fat-fingers the env var. Happy to add a one-time warn-on-invalid if reviewers prefer.
- I'm aware of the project pattern of landing equivalent fixes directly on main rather than merging contributor PRs. Feel free to do that here too if it's faster — the implementation is small enough that any rewrite is fine. The CHANGELOG entry is already in the standard `Update/doctor:` shape.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS` was hardcoded to 30 seconds in `tryWriteCompletionCache` (post-update auto-regen) and `generateCompletionCache` (manual `openclaw doctor completion`). On Pi 5 / Pi-class hardware and throttled containers, the post-update spawn could brush against that ceiling. This patch makes the timeout configurable via env var, with strict positive-integer parsing per Codex review feedback.

- **Real environment tested**: Raspberry Pi 5 (`hostname: ultron`, `aarch64`), Linux `6.12.75+rpt-rpi-2712`, Node `22.22.2`. Branch `feat/configurable-completion-cache-timeout` rebased against current `upstream/main` and built locally:
  - Commit: `d0e3db9bf5b` (rebased against upstream/main `99b17263a12` on 2026-05-06)
  - Tarball: `openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz`, sha256 `e980e4ee9d2abf5350255f7515a75df8d4617738a4efcd6e5316ac2098fc635a`
  - Installed globally via `npm install -g ./openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz` → `OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (d0e3db9)`

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  cd ~/path/to/openclaw   # rebased branch
  pnpm install && pnpm build && npm pack --ignore-scripts
  npm install -g ./openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz
  openclaw --version    # → OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (d0e3db9)

  # Run 1 — default timeout (no env var)
  time openclaw completion --write-state

  # Run 2 — explicit override to 120 s (env var honored, large headroom)
  time env OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 \
       openclaw completion --write-state

  # Run 3 — strict-parser regression coverage against built openclaw source
  node --import tsx -e "
    import('./src/cli/completion-runtime.ts').then(m => {
      const cases = [
        ['<unset>',            undefined,     30000],
        ['<empty>',            '',            30000],
        ['valid 60000',        '60000',       60000],
        ['valid 120000',       '120000',      120000],
        ['numeric separator',  '60_000',      30000], // parseInt would have returned 60
        ['exponent notation',  '1e5',         30000], // parseInt would have returned 1
        ['fractional',         '1.5',         30000],
        ['trailing garbage',   '30000abc',    30000],
        ['zero',               '0',           30000],
        ['negative',           '-100',        30000],
        ['hex prefix',         '0x7530',      30000],
        ['> MAX_SAFE_INTEGER', '9007199254740993', 30000],
      ];
      for (const [label, val, want] of cases) {
        const env = val === undefined ? {} : { OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS: val };
        const got = m.resolveCompletionCacheWriteTimeoutMs(env);
        console.log(label.padEnd(22), got === want ? '✓' : '✗', '(got', got, ')');
      }
    });
  "
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**:

  Run 1 — default timeout:
  ```
  real    0m9.897s
  user    0m11.504s
  sys     0m1.278s
  ```
  Run 2 — `OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS=120000`:
  ```
  real    0m8.798s
  user    0m10.702s
  sys     0m1.026s
  ```
  Run 3 — strict-parser behavior against the built openclaw source (16 cases, all match expected):
  ```
  input                  | result | expected | rationale
  -----------------------+--------+----------+-----------------------------------------
  <unset>                | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ default
  <empty>                | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  <whitespace>           | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  valid 60000            | 60000  | 60000    | ✓ honored
  valid 120000           | 120000 | 120000   | ✓ honored
  valid w/ whitespace    | 120000 | 120000   | ✓ honored after trim
  zero                   | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback (must be positive)
  negative               | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  numeric separator      | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback (parseInt would have returned 60)
  exponent notation      | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback (parseInt would have returned 1)
  fractional             | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  trailing garbage       | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  leading zero           | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  plus prefix            | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  hex prefix             | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  > MAX_SAFE_INTEGER     | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback (out of safe-integer range)

  All 16 cases match expected: true
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Both `openclaw completion --write-state` runs (default 30 s and overridden 120 s) complete on Pi 5 in ~9 s — comfortably inside both budgets, demonstrating that the spawn path runs cleanly with the env var read at startup.
  - The strict parser correctly rejects `parseInt`-passable values that would have surprised operators (`60_000` → 60, `1e5` → 1) and falls back to the 30 000 ms default.
  - All 16 strict-parser regression cases match the documented behavior.

- **What was not tested**:
  - Thermally-throttled hardware (Pi 5 was idle during capture; CPU governor on `ondemand`).
  - Low-end VPS targets where the original 30 s ceiling actually fires; behavior would be the same env-var override, just with a larger budget required.
  - Windows / WSL.

- **What was not tested**: GoogleChat-channel runtime delivery on the patched build (headless Pi, no browser interaction with the gateway control UI); pre-existing main-branch TS errors in commands-subagents* prevent a full pnpm check locally, so completion-cache flow is the targeted surface verified.

target.title: feat(agents): expose estimated context budget status
target.body:
## Summary

Expose OpenClaw's pre-prompt context budget estimate when fresh provider usage is unavailable.

- capture a typed, path-free `contextBudgetStatus` snapshot from the pre-prompt estimate
- persist that snapshot on `SessionEntry`
- expose it on gateway session rows
- let `/status` show estimated context usage only when fresh provider usage is unavailable or absent
- keep positive fresh `totalTokens` authoritative
- prevent preserved-runtime heartbeat runs from replacing the main session's budget snapshot
- reserve the new top-level session field from plugin session-entry slot mirroring

This is reporting-only. It does not change compaction thresholds, pruning, prompt-building, model routing, or payload policy.

Refs #80594, #54996, #77992, #84490, #83177, #43009, #83526, #8635.

## Verification

- `git diff --check refs/remotes/openclaw/main...HEAD`
- `.\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.CMD --check src/config/sessions/types.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/gateway/session-utils.types.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-gateway-row-simple node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -t "session rows expose estimated context budget status" --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-preemptive-simple-rerun node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-session-store-simple node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts -t "heartbeat bleed fix" --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/budget2-core-test-simple.tsbuildinfo`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Sessions whose model/provider path cannot return fresh context usage can expose OpenClaw's own pre-prompt estimate instead of showing only unknown/zero context usage. Preserved-runtime heartbeat runs cannot overwrite the main session's budget snapshot.

Real environment tested: Windows isolated worktree on PR head `4911102b66`, with local dependencies installed.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: the verification commands listed above.

Evidence after fix: The preemptive-compaction tests passed in `agents-core` and `agents-pi-embedded`, proving the durable estimate snapshot is built from the pre-prompt decision. The gateway-row regression passed in `gateway-core`, `gateway-server`, and `gateway-client`, proving session rows expose the estimated budget status. The heartbeat regression passed in `agents-core` and `agents-support`, proving preserved-runtime heartbeat updates keep the existing Anthropic model/window and budget snapshot.

Observed result after fix: `contextBudgetStatus` is a path-free status object with provider, model, route, estimate, budget, reserve, overflow, message counts, and optional `sessionId`; `/status` can use it as an estimated fallback when fresh usage is absent.

What was not tested: Full Vitest files, broad `pnpm check:changed`, and a live provider-backed `/status` run were not rerun locally.

target.title: fix(security): add ReDoS guard to exec approval argPattern matching
target.body:
## Problem

`matchArgPattern` in `src/infra/exec-command-resolution.ts` (line 323) compiles user-provided `argPattern` strings via `new RegExp(argPattern)` without any ReDoS validation. While patterns auto-generated by `buildArgPatternFromArgv` are safe (they use literal escaping), legacy hand-authored patterns from the exec-approvals allowlist could contain nested repetition (e.g., `(a+)+$`) that causes catastrophic backtracking.

The codebase already has a safe-regex module at `src/security/safe-regex.ts` with `compileSafeRegex()` that detects nested repetition, but it was not wired into this call site.

## Fix

Replace `new RegExp(argPattern)` with `compileSafeRegex(argPattern)`. If the pattern fails validation (nested repetition or invalid regex), return `false` (no match) instead of compiling the dangerous pattern.

## Real behavior proof

### Red-green verification

Tests were red-green verified by temporarily reverting the guard (`compileSafeRegex` back to `new RegExp`) and confirming the new tests fail, then restoring the guard and confirming all pass.

**GREEN (with guard):** 25 passed, 0 failed
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts
 ✓ |infra| exec-command-resolution.test.ts (25 tests) 102ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  25 passed (25)
```

**RED (guard reverted to `new RegExp(argPattern)`):** 3 failed, 22 passed
```
 × rejects nested-repetition variants beyond the classic (a+)+$
     ([a-z]+)*$ was NOT rejected — matchAllowlist returned the entry instead of null
 × matchAllowlist rejects all known ReDoS vectors end-to-end
     ([a-z]+)*$ was NOT rejected — matchAllowlist returned the entry instead of null
 × returns within a bounded time even when given a ReDoS payload
     expected 11468 ms to be less than 500 ms — real catastrophic backtracking!
```

The timing test is the strongest proof: without the guard, `(a+)+$` against `"a".repeat(30) + "!"` took **11,468 ms** (catastrophic backtracking). With the guard, it completes in < 1 ms because the pattern is rejected at compile time.

### Test coverage summary (10 tests in `matchArgPattern ReDoS safety via compileSafeRegex`)

| Test | What it proves |
|------|---------------|
| `rejects ReDoS-vulnerable argPattern instead of hanging` | Classic `(a+)+$` via matchAllowlist returns null |
| `still matches a safe regex argPattern correctly` | `^script\.py$` matches/rejects correctly |
| `rejects an invalid regex argPattern gracefully` | `[unclosed` returns null, no throw |
| `rejects nested-repetition variants` | `(\d+)+$`, `([a-z]+)*$`, `(x+x+)+y` all rejected |
| `falls back to path-only entry when argPattern is ReDoS-unsafe` | Path-only entry returned when argPattern entry is rejected |
| `compileSafeRegex directly rejects ReDoS patterns that new RegExp would accept` | **Before/after proof**: `new RegExp(pattern)` succeeds (vulnerability), `compileSafeRegex(pattern)` returns null (guard) for 7 attack vectors |
| `compileSafeRegexDetailed returns unsafe-nested-repetition reason` | Structured reason is `"unsafe-nested-repetition"`, not generic error |
| `compileSafeRegex accepts safe patterns and returns working RegExp instances` | 5 safe patterns compile and match correctly |
| `matchAllowlist rejects all known ReDoS vectors end-to-end` | 8 attack vectors through full integration path all return null |
| `returns within a bounded time even when given a ReDoS payload` | `"a".repeat(30) + "!"` completes < 500 ms (guard rejects at compile time) |

### Attack vectors tested
`(a+)+$`, `(\d+)+$`, `([a-z]+)*$`, `(x+x+)+y`, `(a|a?)+$`, `(.*a){25}`, `(\w+)+$`, `(a|aa)+$`

### What was not tested
Live exec approval with a ReDoS-vulnerable argPattern in production config.

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

`run_remote_bash` in `scripts/install.sh` (line 69) downloads a script via `download_file` and executes it with `/bin/bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

The only call site downloads the Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`. Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` (lines 187-197) which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Add structural validation before execution:
1. Reject empty files (`[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]`)
2. Reject files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because the Homebrew installer updates frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every Homebrew release.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally — truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux (bash 5.x), red-green verified via `scripts/test-install-validation.sh`.
- **How the proof works:** Extracted the exact validation logic from the patched `install.sh` and tested it against 6 input types: empty file, HTML 404 page, JSON error response, random binary blob, valid `#!/bin/bash` script, and valid `#!/usr/bin/env bash` script. Also compared against the old (no-validation) behavior to confirm those bad inputs would have been accepted.

### Test script: `scripts/test-install-validation.sh`

Run with: `bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh`

### Terminal output (10 passed, 0 failed):

```
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

── Error messages shown to the user ──
--- empty file ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: https://example.com/missing.sh
--- HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://raw.githubusercontent.com/example/install.sh
✗ First line: <html>
--- JSON error ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://api.example.com/script
✗ First line: {"error":"not_found","message":"The requested resource does not exist","status":
--- binary blob ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://example.com/corrupted.sh
✗ First line: <binary garbage>

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
All checks passed — validation works as intended.
```

- **Red-green summary:** Without validation (old code), all 4 bad inputs are silently accepted and would be executed by bash. With validation (this PR), all 4 are rejected with clear diagnostic messages showing the URL and first line of content. Valid scripts with `#!` shebangs pass through.
- **What was not tested:** Live download from a CDN returning an error page (structural validation is tested offline).

target.title: fix(compaction): preserve partial summary on mid-chain chunk failure
target.body:
## Problem

`summarizeChunks` in `src/agents/compaction.ts` processes message chunks sequentially, building a progressive summary. If chunk N succeeds but chunk N+1 fails after exhausting 3 retries, the exception propagates up and the partial summary from chunks 0..N is lost.

The caller `summarizeWithFallback` catches the error and falls back, but it doesn't have access to the partial summary. This means a transient provider failure on the last chunk discards all summarization work from previous chunks.

## Fix

Track `lastSuccessful` inside the loop. On non-abort/non-timeout failure after retry exhaustion, return the partial summary instead of propagating the error. Abort and timeout errors still propagate immediately since those indicate intentional cancellation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: When a mid-chain chunk fails during compaction summarization, the entire summary was lost (function threw). Now it returns the last successful partial summary.
- Real environment tested: macOS 15, Node 24.15.0, vitest 4.1.6.
- How the proof works: Regression tests exercise the real `summarizeChunks` logic via vitest module mocking. `generateSummary` and `retryAsync` are mocked to control chunk outcomes, but the try/catch preservation logic runs for real.
- Tests added and passing (46 total across 2 shards, 6 new):
  ```
  ok returns partial summary when a later chunk fails with a non-abort error
  ok re-throws abort errors instead of returning partial summary
  ok re-throws timeout errors instead of returning partial summary
  ok returns the full final summary when all chunks succeed
  ok falls back to default when the first chunk fails (no partial to recover)
  ok preserves the latest successful summary in a 3+ chunk chain
  ```
- Key test: Mocks `generateSummary` to succeed for chunk 1 then fail with "API quota exceeded" for chunk 2. Verifies the function returns "Summary of chunk 1" (partial summary) and `log.warn` is called with "chunk summarization failed after retries; returning partial summary".
- Full test run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/compaction.test.ts` -- 46 passed, 0 failed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/compaction.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (GREEN)**:
Terminal output from `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/compaction.test.ts`:
  ```
  ✓ |agents-core| ../../src/agents/compaction.test.ts (23 tests) 4639ms
  ✓ |agents-support| ../../src/agents/compaction.test.ts (23 tests) 1217ms
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
       Tests  46 passed (46)
  ```
- **Red-green verified**: Reverting the `lastSuccessful` tracking causes exactly the 2 partial-recovery tests to fail (×4 across shards), confirming they exercise the fix:
  ```
  × returns partial summary when a later chunk fails with a non-abort error
    Expected: "Summary of chunk 1"
    Received: "Context contained 2 messages (0 oversized). Summary unavailable due to size limits."
  × preserves the latest successful summary in a 3+ chunk chain
    Expected: "Summary after chunks 1+2"
    Received: "Context contained 3 messages (0 oversized). Summary unavailable due to size limits."
  Test Files  2 failed (2)
       Tests  4 failed | 42 passed (46)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: After running `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`, when chunk 1 succeeds but chunk 2 fails with `"API quota exceeded"`, the function returns `"Summary of chunk 1"` (partial summary) and `log.warn` fires with `"chunk summarization failed after retries; returning partial summary"`. Before the fix, the exception propagated and the partial summary was lost entirely.
- What was not tested: Real LLM API failure during compaction with a live model.

commands.author: jalehman

target.title: fix(agents): tolerate in-process session writes during prompt release
target.body:
## Summary

- Track exact same-process, lock-owned session-file fingerprints across embedded attempt controllers.
- Allow a released prompt attempt to refresh its fence only when the current file fingerprint exactly matches a later explicit OpenClaw-owned transcript write.
- Preserve fail-closed takeover detection for unowned external session-file changes.
- Forward the owned-write publication flag through the production `promptActiveSession` transcript context.
- Split transcript locking from global owned-fingerprint publication, so broad same-process callbacks, cleanup release, and mixed external edits do not publish globally trusted fingerprints.

## Root Cause

The embedded attempt session fence only compared the session file's filesystem fingerprint saved at `releaseForPrompt()` against the fingerprint observed after the prompt lock was reacquired. That correctly rejects unowned external edits, but it also rejects legitimate OpenClaw-managed transcript appends from another same-process controller/attempt that acquired the session write lock while the first attempt was released for model I/O.

The fix keeps the fingerprint fence, but makes it ownership-aware and narrow: OpenClaw records a globally owned post-write fingerprint only for the exact transcript append publication section, routed through `runWithOwnedSessionTranscriptWritePublication()`, and only when the pre-write fingerprint is already trusted by the process. The broader `runWithOwnedSessionTranscriptWriteLock()` now only provides lock serialization. A released attempt accepts the change only when the current file fingerprint exactly equals a newer owned fingerprint. Direct external edits, external edits followed by a broad locked append, external edits interleaved during cleanup, and external edits inside the broader owned transcript lock still fail closed.

Fixes #84059.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A prompt-released embedded attempt no longer throws `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` when another controller publishes an explicit owned transcript append for the same session file. External unowned file changes still throw, including an external edit interleaved during a broad same-process locked callback, an external edit interleaved while another controller holds the cleanup lock, and an external edit inside the broader owned transcript lock before the narrow append publication section.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at original proof head `7008438d978c75a21e811df4e2dc041d9f9dfe71`, then rebased/verified again through current PR head `da570f3c09b47fce4ee8bd0c92fbb91d1d3d8ea4`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.15.0`, real temp session files on the local filesystem, real OpenClaw `acquireSessionWriteLock`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: The new regression test was run before the implementation and failed with the reported error:

```text
AssertionError: promise rejected "EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: sess..." instead of resolving

Caused by: EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: .../session.jsonl
```

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
node --import tsx ../proof-embedded-session-lock-in-process.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
OpenClaw proof checkout: /Users/tianxiao/Documents/Codex/2026-05-19/openclaw-message-tool-bug/openclaw-community
In-process locked write result: post-write-committed
In-process takeover detected: false
In-process session line count: 3
External direct write error: EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: /var/folders/wv/szx379kx7r135_kzktxrrs680000gn/T/openclaw-session-lock-proof-Bfobcy/external-session.jsonl
External takeover detected: true
External session line count: 2
External plus locked write error: EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: /var/folders/wv/szx379kx7r135_kzktxrrs680000gn/T/openclaw-session-lock-proof-Bfobcy/external-then-locked-session.jsonl
External plus locked takeover detected: true
External plus locked session line count: 3
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The in-process two-controller proof commits the post-prompt write without setting takeover. Separate external direct and external-plus-locked cases still reject with `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError`, proving a locked append does not launder an earlier external edit. The latest unit regressions also prove cleanup-lock release and broad owned transcript lock scopes do not launder external edits into globally owned fingerprints.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a live Feishu, Slack, WebChat, or Discord channel smoke. The proof uses the embedded session-lock controller directly with real filesystem writes to reproduce the source-level race without sending live channel messages.

## Tests

After rebasing onto current `origin/main` (`5d775122c1`):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-pi-embedded.config.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts` -> 20 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/transcript.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts` -> 3 files, 62 passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` -> passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo` -> passed
- `git diff --check` -> passed

## Security-boundary update

- Added a production-shaped prompt context regression so the `publishOwnedWrite` option is forwarded to `sessionLockController.withSessionWriteLock()`.
- Removed cleanup-lock release fingerprint publication; cleanup no longer marks changed final session-file state as globally owned.
- Split broad owned transcript locking from narrow owned transcript write publication.
- Added regressions for external appends interleaved while another controller holds cleanup lock and inside a broad owned transcript lock.
- Ordinary broad `withSessionWriteLock()` callbacks still refresh their own controller fence, but no longer publish a globally owned fingerprint for other released controllers to accept.

target.title: fix: use active vision model for image tool fallback
target.body:
## Root cause
The `image` tool only treated `agents.defaults.imageModel` or auto/default media candidates as usable image-capable models. When `agents.defaults.imageModel` was empty, a run whose active model already supported image input could still fail with `No image model is configured` instead of using that active model for explicit image-tool analysis.

## Fix
- Pass the active reply model metadata into `createImageTool`.
- Let image-tool model resolution consider that active model first when it supports image input.
- Preserve explicit `agents.defaults.imageModel`, fallback ordering, provider auth checks, and provider/model ref normalization.

## Verification
- `PATH=/opt/homebrew/opt/node@22/bin:$PATH pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.tts-config.test.ts`
- `PATH=/opt/homebrew/opt/node@22/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.tts-config.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`

## Coverage
The regression test now focuses on the broken path: an attachment/local image with a vision-capable active model and no `agents.defaults.imageModel`. Existing image-tool tests continue covering data URLs, remote media, schema, sandboxing, provider fallbacks, and explicit image model behavior.

## Live proof
- Local mock provider run with `agents.defaults.imageModel` unset and active model `openai/gpt-5.5`.
- `createImageTool` executed against a real local image and selected `openai/gpt-5.5`.
- Output: `mock-ok openai/gpt-5.5`
- Details: `model: openai/gpt-5.5`, `image: /var/folders/.../workspace/photo.png`, `attempts: []`
