target.title: fix(codex): keep interrupted turns visible-answer eligible
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex app-server `turn.status: "interrupted"` was projected as an OpenClaw abort even when OpenClaw did not explicitly cancel the run.
- Solution: Keep app-server interrupted as terminal app-server state, but do not map it to OpenClaw `aborted` unless OpenClaw explicitly called `markAborted()` or `markTimedOut()`.
- What changed: Added projector and runner regressions for interrupted/tool-only/no-visible-answer Codex turns, including sparse successful bash output.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This did not treat every interrupted turn as success, did not synthesize final assistant text, did not disable cancellation, and did not change failed-turn error handling.

## Motivation

- Codex-backed dashboard turns can appear to stop after tool output without delivering the final visible assistant answer.
- The existing incomplete-turn/no-visible-answer guard was bypassed because the app-server `interrupted` terminal status was mapped to `aborted: true`.
- This materially affects UX for Codex-backed OpenClaw usage: the user sees tool-only progress and then needs a continuation turn instead of receiving the answer that the original user-facing turn owed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84492
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex app-server interrupted turn status no longer suppresses no-visible-answer handling for user-facing turns.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw dev checkout rebased on `origin/main` plus Crabbox static SSH Linux VM. Private host/user/workroot values were redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```shell
crabbox run --provider ssh --target linux --static-host <redacted-lab-host> \
  --static-user <redacted-user> --static-port 22 \
  --static-work-root <redacted-workroot> --shell -- \
  'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"; node --version; pnpm --version;
   pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts;
   CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts \
     extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts \
     -t "app-server interrupted status|sparse successful bash output";
   CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-pi-embedded.config.ts \
     src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts \
     -t "sparse bash output"'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```shell
event-projector.test.ts: Test Files 1 passed; Tests 2 passed | 58 skipped
run.incomplete-turn.test.ts: Test Files 1 passed; Tests 1 passed | 95 skipped
run summary sync=2.536s command=32.891s total=35.452s exit=0
lease cleanup stopped=true policy=auto
```

- Observed result after fix: The focused interrupted-turn and sparse-bash regressions passed remotely through Crabbox.
- What was not tested: A full dashboard browser interaction was not run. Full monorepo tests were not run.
- Before evidence:

```shell
FAIL extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts > CodexAppServerEventProjector > does not treat app-server interrupted status as a user cancellation by itself
AssertionError: expected true to be false
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `CodexAppServerEventProjector` converted `turn.status === "interrupted"` into `this.aborted = true` and returned `aborted: this.aborted || turnInterrupted`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no regression covering an app-server interrupted terminal turn with no explicit OpenClaw cancellation and no visible assistant answer.
- Contributing context (if known): The embedded runner intentionally skips incomplete-turn/no-visible-answer handling for true aborts. That made the projector's over-broad status mapping user-visible.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - App-server `interrupted` does not by itself become OpenClaw `aborted`.
  - Sparse successful bash output plus no visible final assistant text remains eligible for no-visible-answer handling.
  - Replay-unsafe shell activity surfaces a verification warning instead of silently retrying or faking an answer.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug was a local projection/lifecycle mapping problem plus runner incomplete-turn classification; the focused tests cover both without live model nondeterminism.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None found.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

User-facing Codex turns that end with app-server `interrupted` but no explicit OpenClaw cancellation can now reach the existing no-visible-answer guard instead of being classified as aborted.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Codex turn/completed(status=interrupted) -> projector aborted=true -> incomplete-turn guard skipped -> no final visible answer handling

After:
Codex turn/completed(status=interrupted) -> projector aborted unchanged -> incomplete-turn guard remains active -> retry/error handling stays visible
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux x64 local dev and Linux x64 Crabbox SSH VM
- Runtime/container: Node `v24.15.0` locally and on the VM
- Model/provider: Codex app-server/OpenAI Codex path modeled by focused projector tests
- Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw Codex app-server harness
- Relevant config (redacted): Crabbox static SSH provider with redacted host/user/workroot

### Steps

1. Rebased the branch on latest official `origin/main`.
2. Ran the focused local test files.
3. Ran the focused regression selections remotely through Crabbox static SSH.

### Expected

- App-server `interrupted` did not automatically become OpenClaw `aborted`.
- Sparse successful bash output did not count as a final visible assistant answer.
- Replay-unsafe tool activity surfaced the existing verification warning path.

### Actual

- Matched expected after this patch.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local focused tests after rebase:

```shell
CI=1 timeout 240s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  60 passed (60)
```

```shell
CI=1 timeout 240s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-pi-embedded.config.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  96 passed (96)
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Pre-fix focused regression failed on current `main`.
  - Local focused files passed after the patch and after rebase.
  - Crabbox SSH remote proof passed after rebase.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Explicit `markAborted()`/`markTimedOut()` paths remained separate.
  - Failed turns still set `promptError`.
  - Replay-unsafe sparse bash output surfaced a verification warning instead of being silently retried.
- What was not verified:
  - Full dashboard browser UX.
  - Full monorepo test suite.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Some app-server `interrupted` statuses may correspond to genuine app-server-side interruptions.
  - Mitigation: The patch does not mark them successful and does not fake a reply; it only avoids treating them as OpenClaw/user aborts. Explicit OpenClaw abort and timeout paths still set `aborted`/`promptError`.

<!-- exact-interrupted-tool-only-proof-2026-05-20 -->

### Exact interrupted tool-only recovery proof

Added diagnostic regression coverage at commit `2010177bc7` for the exact path ClawSweeper requested:

- Codex app-server terminal projection: `turn/completed status=interrupted`.
- Prior turn item: successful bash `commandExecution` with empty aggregated output and `exitCode=0`.
- No assistant text was projected: `assistantTexts=[]`.
- OpenClaw projection after the patch: `aborted=false`, `timedOut=false`, bash tool metadata preserved.
- The no-visible-answer guard was exercised with a projector-compatible interrupted tool-only attempt and produced recovery text containing `couldn't generate a response`.
- Explicit cancellation counter-proof remained distinct: an explicitly aborted interrupted/tool-only attempt returned `null` from `resolveIncompleteTurnPayloadText(...)`, so user cancellation does not synthesize a fake final answer.

Focused proof commands run locally:

```bash
CI=1 timeout 240s /home/probo/.local/share/pnpm/store/v11/links/@/node/24.15.0/73ce00437ab4f9c458daa7174613927ead616d9a77c35c8b15545d4dc2ac94a0/node_modules/node/bin/node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts
# Test Files 1 passed (1)
# Tests 61 passed (61)

CI=1 timeout 240s /home/probo/.local/share/pnpm/store/v11/links/@/node/24.15.0/73ce00437ab4f9c458daa7174613927ead616d9a77c35c8b15545d4dc2ac94a0/node_modules/node/bin/node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-pi-embedded.config.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts -t "app-server interrupted tool-only output"
# Test Files 1 passed (1)
# Tests 1 passed | 96 skipped (97)

CI=1 timeout 240s /home/probo/.local/share/pnpm/store/v11/links/@/node/24.15.0/73ce00437ab4f9c458daa7174613927ead616d9a77c35c8b15545d4dc2ac94a0/node_modules/node/bin/node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -t "keeps upstream cancellation aborted"
# Test Files 1 passed (1)
# Tests 1 passed | 185 skipped (186)
```

Crabbox SSH proof on the lab VM also passed for the exact diagnostic pair:

```bash
CRABBOX_CONFIG=/home/probo/.config/crabbox/lab-ssh.yaml CRABBOX_SSH_KEY=/home/probo/.ssh/crabbox_lab_ed25519 timeout 1200s /home/probo/.local/bin/crabbox run --provider ssh --target linux --static-host [redacted] --static-user [redacted] --static-port 22 --static-work-root /home/[redacted]/crabbox --shell -- 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"; CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts -t "sparse successful bash output|explicit cancellation marked aborted"; CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-pi-embedded.config.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts -t "app-server interrupted tool-only output"'
# event-projector: Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 2 passed | 59 skipped (61)
# incomplete-turn: Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 1 passed | 96 skipped (97)
```

<!-- exact-runtime-interrupted-tool-only-proof-2026-05-20 -->

### Exact runtime interrupted tool-only recovery proof

After the focused tests, I also ran a runtime diagnostic through the real OpenClaw modules from commit `2010177bc7` rather than a Vitest assertion-only path. The script instantiated `CodexAppServerEventProjector`, fed a redacted Codex app-server `turn/completed` notification with `status="interrupted"`, one successful `commandExecution` item, `aggregatedOutput=""`, and `exitCode=0`, then passed the projected attempt into `resolveIncompleteTurnPayloadText(...)`.


This is the exact after-fix runtime path requested by ClawSweeper: app-server `interrupted`, tool-only/no-assistant, no explicit OpenClaw abort, no timeout, no synthesized assistant answer, and the existing no-visible-answer recovery text remained reachable. target.title: fix(ui): exec approval modal closes without submitting decision
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Clicking "Allow once" (or any decision button) in the exec approval modal closes the modal but silently fails to submit the approval to the backend. The command stays stuck at "Approval required" indefinitely.
- Why it matters: Users lose the ability to approve tool executions through the Control UI, forcing them to use CLI workarounds.
- What changed: The approval buttons now pass the active approval ID directly to the handler via the click closure, instead of the handler reading from `execApprovalQueue[0]` at call time.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No backend changes, no new state, no new dependencies. The handler signature adds an optional parameter with full backward compat.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #55251
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: Race condition between the expiry cleanup timer and the button click handler. When `exec.approval.requested` arrives, the event handler sets a `setTimeout` to auto-remove the entry from `execApprovalQueue` after expiry. If this timer fires between the last Lit render and the user's click, the handler reads `this.execApprovalQueue[0]` as `undefined` and returns silently — no request is sent, but the modal disappears on the next render cycle because the queue is now empty.
- Missing detection / guardrail: The handler had no fallback for the case where the queue entry was already pruned but the button DOM was still visible.
- Prior context: The expiry timer was added to clean up stale approval entries, but the handler was written assuming the queue would always be in sync with the rendered DOM at click time.
- Why this regressed now: Likely more noticeable in environments with clock skew between gateway and client (e.g., VM port-forwarding setups where the gateway clock and browser clock diverge), which shortens the effective timer delay.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: Backend approval logic is correct — `exec.approval.resolve` properly validates and resolves pending IDs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.ts` render output
- Scenario the test should lock in: Button click handler passes the approval ID from the rendered entry, not from live queue state.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The race only manifests when the queue state diverges from the rendered DOM, which a unit test can simulate by clearing the queue before invoking the handler.
- If no new test is added, why not: The existing test infrastructure for the approval modal does not cover click handler arguments. Adding a targeted test for this would require mocking the Lit render cycle, which is out of scope for this fix.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The approval modal buttons now reliably submit the decision to the backend, even if the approval entry expires from the queue between render and click.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user clicks Allow once] -> handler reads queue[0] -> undefined (timer cleared it) -> silent return -> modal disappears -> command stuck

After:
[user clicks Allow once] -> handler receives approval ID from closure -> sends resolve request -> command proceeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22+, npm global install
- Model/provider: any
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI (webchat)
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.elevated.enabled: true`

### Steps

1. Trigger any command that requires exec approval
2. Wait for the approval modal to appear in Control UI
3. Click "Allow once"

### Expected

- Approval is submitted, command proceeds

### Actual (before fix)

- Modal closes, no request sent, command stuck at "Approval required"

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets — verified that `exec.approval.resolve` request is now sent via WebSocket after clicking "Allow once", confirmed via browser DevTools WS frame inspector

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Read through the full approval flow from button click → handler → WebSocket request → backend resolve → broadcast event. Confirmed the fix correctly passes the ID from the render-time closure.
- Edge cases checked: Handler still works without the optional parameter (backward compat). Handler still correctly handles expired/unknown IDs (backend returns error, catch block displays it).

target.title: fix(gateway): run daily session resets on schedule
target.body:
## Summary

- Daily session resets now run from gateway maintenance instead of waiting for the next inbound session use.
- The scheduler scans configured session stores and calls the existing reset service for stale daily sessions after the configured boundary.
- The maintenance timer now reads current runtime config on each sweep, so hot config changes to `session.reset`, `session.resetByType`, `session.resetByChannel`, or store targets are applied without a gateway restart.
- Docs now describe proactive daily rollover and the implicit provider-owned CLI session exemption.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra
- [x] Docs

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #82321
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

A daily reset with `atHour` is a wall-clock policy. Users should not need to send a new message after the reset boundary before stale session context is cleared. The maintenance sweep should also honor the current runtime config after hot reloads, not the startup snapshot.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Daily sessions stale past `atHour` are reset by gateway maintenance without waiting for a new inbound message, while active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions are preserved.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout in a Linux container, on branch `fix-daily-session-reset`, running the patched gateway maintenance timer against an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. Bundled plugin facades were disabled for the proof process with `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1` so the reset service did not depend on source-checkout plugin artifacts.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Ran a live Node command that starts `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` with the patched daily reset scheduler, current-config getter, real `performGatewaySessionReset` path, isolated temp session store, one stale daily session, one stale active-run session, and one implicit provider-owned CLI session.
  - Command shape: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... startGatewayMaintenanceTimers(...) ... TS`
  - Supplemental verification: `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts`
  - Supplemental type gates: `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- Evidence after fix:
  ```text
  $ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # creates isolated agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  # starts patched gateway maintenance timers
  # waits for daily reset sweep to invoke the real reset service
  TS
  gateway-proof: before {"stale":"old-regular-session","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  [session-daily-reset] daily session reset sweep complete
  gateway-proof: after {"stale":"5f959c12-f0ed-4ed1-99d2-cc158c66ac87","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  gateway-proof: staleReset true
  gateway-proof: activePreserved true
  gateway-proof: implicitCliPreserved true

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests       18 passed (18)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The running gateway maintenance timer reset the stale daily session through the actual reset service without any inbound message. The active-run session and implicit provider-owned CLI session kept their original session IDs. Focused coverage also verifies fresh sessions are left alone and later sweeps use current runtime config after reset policy changes.
- What was not tested: A multi-hour live gateway wait that naturally crosses the configured reset hour.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

Daily reset freshness was only evaluated through session-use paths. If no message arrived after `atHour`, the stale session could remain active even though the configured reset time had passed. The initial scheduler also captured the startup config, which meant hot reloads could leave the sweep using stale reset policy until restart.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - A stale daily session resets during a scheduler sweep without a new message.
  - A fresh daily session is left alone.
  - Implicit provider-owned CLI sessions are not cut by the default daily policy.
  - Sessions with active runs are skipped.
  - Scheduler sweeps use current runtime config after a reset-policy change.
- Existing coverage before this patch covered stale detection when called, but not proactive scheduled execution or reload-aware maintenance behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Configured daily session resets now happen on schedule during gateway uptime, even if no message arrives after the reset boundary. The public docs now state that proactive rollover can happen from gateway maintenance and that implicit provider-owned CLI sessions keep continuity unless reset is explicit.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
daily reset boundary
  -> gateway maintenance sweep
  -> read current runtime config
  -> skip active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions
  -> stale daily sessions reset through existing reset service
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux container
- Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: `session.reset.mode=daily`, `session.reset.atHour=4`

### Steps

1. Create a session store entry with `sessionStartedAt` before the daily reset boundary.
2. Run the daily reset scheduler scan with `nowMs` after the reset boundary.
3. Confirm the reset callback runs for the stale session.
4. Repeat with a fresh post-boundary session and confirm it is not reset.
5. Mark a stale session key active and confirm it is skipped.
6. Add provider-owned CLI continuity under the implicit reset policy and confirm it is skipped.
7. Change the scheduler's runtime config from a noon reset boundary to a 4 AM reset boundary and confirm the next sweep uses the new policy.

### Expected

Stale daily sessions reset without inbound traffic; fresh, active, and implicitly provider-owned CLI sessions remain unchanged; config reloads affect future scheduler sweeps.

### Actual

The focused scheduler and gateway maintenance tests match that behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Focused scheduler and maintenance test output.
- Test and production typecheck output.
- Docs updated in:
  - `docs/concepts/session.md`
  - `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`
  - `docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: stale daily reset scheduler path, fresh-session no-op path, active-run skip, implicit provider-owned CLI skip, runtime-config reload path, gateway maintenance timer lifecycle.
- Edge cases checked: scheduler skips overlapping sweeps; shutdown/runtime cleanup clears the timer; type gates pass.
- Not verified: a live gateway instance naturally crossing the configured reset hour.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the gateway now scans configured session stores every minute.
- Mitigation: scans are bounded to discovered session stores, skip overlapping runs, and only call reset for stale daily sessions.
- Risk: deployments that assumed lazy daily resets may observe earlier reset behavior.
- Mitigation: the new behavior matches the configured wall-clock expectation for `mode: daily` and `atHour`.
- Risk: hot config changes could otherwise make the scheduler use stale reset policy.
- Mitigation: future sweeps call the gateway runtime config getter before scanning.

target.title: fix: detect zombie processes on macOS to prevent stale session locks
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** On macOS, when a gateway process is killed but still exists as a zombie, `process.kill(pid, 0)` succeeds, causing `isPidAlive()` to return `true`. This prevented stale session locks from being reclaimed after gateway restarts, blocking the bot from responding to messages.
- **Fix:** Added macOS zombie detection using `ps -o state= -p <pid>` which returns `Z` or `Z+` for zombie processes.

## Root Cause

The `isZombieProcess()` function only worked on Linux by reading `/proc/<pid>/status`. On macOS, it always returned `false`, so zombie processes were incorrectly reported as alive.

## Changes

1. Refactored `isZombieProcess()` into platform-specific functions:
   - `isZombieProcessLinux()`: reads `/proc/<pid>/status` (existing logic)
   - `isZombieProcessMac()`: uses `ps -o state= -p <pid>` to check for `Z` or `Z+` state
2. Added test for macOS zombie detection code path

## Testing

- All existing tests pass
- Added new test for macOS zombie detection

## Linked Issues

Fixes #32799, #49035, #29927

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

On macOS, stale session locks from killed gateway processes will now be automatically reclaimed, preventing the bot from becoming unresponsive after gateway restarts.

target.title: fix: use StringDecoder for UTF-8 boundary-safe chunk decoding in child adapter
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #50887

During streaming responses, multi-byte UTF-8 characters (e.g. Chinese) can get corrupted with U+FFFD replacement characters. This happens in the `child.ts` adapter where Node.js stdio stream chunks are decoded with `chunk.toString()` independently.

## Root Cause

When a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence (e.g. a 3-byte Chinese character) is split across two consecutive Buffer chunks from the OS, calling `chunk.toString()` on each chunk independently causes:
- Chunk 1: partial bytes → decoded as U+FFFD
- Chunk 2: remaining bytes → decoded as U+FFFD

## Fix

Replace `chunk.toString()` with `StringDecoder.write(chunk)` which:
1. Buffers incomplete multi-byte sequences at chunk boundaries
2. Emits only complete code points per chunk
3. Flushes any remaining partial sequence on stream `end`

This matches the pattern already used in `parseNdjsonStream` (ollama-stream.ts) and `web-shared.ts` where `decoder.decode(value, { stream: true })` is used correctly.

## Changes

- `src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.ts`: Import `StringDecoder` from `node:string_decoder`; create per-stream decoder instances; use `decoder.write(chunk)` for Buffer chunks; flush with `decoder.end()` on stream end

## Testing

The fix is low-risk (StringDecoder is a Node.js built-in with identical semantics for ASCII/single-byte content) and restores correct behavior for multi-byte UTF-8 streaming output.

target.title: feat: surface session label/displayName in /status and TUI
target.body:
## Summary

Shows friendly session labels instead of raw UUID-heavy session keys in the `/status` command and TUI status summary.

**Before:**
```
- agent:main:cron:b03cbb21-9519-416c-8af0-effe954ae672:run:446baffc... [direct] | 25m ago | model claude-opus-4-6 | tokens 15k/1.0m
```

**After:**
```
- Cron: claude-keepalive [direct] | 25m ago | model claude-opus-4-6 | tokens 15k/1.0m
```

## Changes

The session store already has `label` and `displayName` fields (set by cron jobs, subagents via `sessions_spawn`, etc.) but they weren't being passed through to the status display layer.

This adds them to the pipeline:

1. **`SessionStatus` type** — added optional `label` and `displayName` fields
2. **Status summary builder** — reads `label`/`displayName` from session store entries
3. **CLI status command** — prefers `displayName → label → key` for the Key column
4. **TUI status summary** — same fallback chain for the session list
5. **TUI client types** — added fields to `GatewayStatusSummary` recent session type

Fallback chain ensures raw keys still display when no label is set.

Closes #51184

target.title: feat(gigachat): add provider integration
target.body:
# feat(gigachat): add provider integration

## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw did not have a bundled GigaChat provider, and GigaChat cannot be represented as a plain static API-key provider because Sber requires OAuth token exchange from an Authorization key.
- Why it matters: Users with GigaChat access need a first-class provider path that preserves OpenClaw auth profiles, TLS/proxy request policy, model catalog, docs, and failover behavior.
- What changed: Added the bundled `gigachat` provider plugin with static text model catalog, Authorization-key onboarding, scoped OAuth runtime token exchange, endpoint selection, custom-tool payload adaptation, legacy `function_call` stream parsing, docs, labeler coverage, generated plugin reference updates, and focused tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Did not add GigaChat image/file/audio adapters, built-in GigaChat functions, dynamic model discovery, live credential migration, or broad provider policy changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix

- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Auth / tokens

- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only. Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes; terminal screenshots and copied live output count. Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

- Behavior addressed: Adds GigaChat provider registration, onboarding metadata, catalog defaults, OAuth runtime auth exchange, custom-tool payload adaptation, legacy `function_call` stream parsing, docs, and labeler/docs navigation coverage.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS Darwin 25.3.0 source checkout on branch `gigachat-provider`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/gigachat/index.test.ts extensions/gigachat/runtime-auth.test.ts extensions/gigachat/stream.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`; `pnpm format:check -- src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts extensions/gigachat/index.ts extensions/gigachat/index.test.ts extensions/gigachat/openclaw.plugin.json extensions/gigachat/stream.ts extensions/gigachat/stream.test.ts docs/providers/gigachat.md .artifacts/gigachat-provider-pr.md`; `node scripts/generate-plugin-inventory-doc.mjs --check`; `git diff --check`; `scripts/committer "feat(gigachat): add provider integration" ...`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Targeted Vitest output reported `Test Files 5 passed (5)` and `Tests 349 passed (349)`. Targeted format check reported `All matched files use the correct format`. Plugin inventory check and `git diff --check` exited successfully. Commit hook formatted staged files and created `69ca0e22c4 feat(gigachat): add provider integration`.
- Observed result after fix: The provider plugin registers, exposes the expected auth choice and catalog, applies onboarding defaults, selects the business endpoint from plugin config, adapts OpenAI-style custom tools to GigaChat `functions/function_call`, parses streamed legacy `function_call` chunks into OpenClaw tool calls, classifies GigaChat rate/context errors, and exchanges Authorization keys for Bearer tokens in mocked runtime-auth tests.
- What was not tested: Live GigaChat OAuth/chat request was not tested because `GIGACHAT_AUTHORIZATION_KEY` was not present in env or `~/.profile`, and no trusted GigaChat root certificate path was available. Broad Testbox/Crabbox changed gate was not run because `crabbox`/`blacksmith` were unavailable in this environment.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): N/A for a new provider surface.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A.
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test

- Target test or file: `extensions/gigachat/runtime-auth.test.ts`; `extensions/gigachat/index.test.ts`; `extensions/gigachat/stream.test.ts`; `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: Runtime OAuth request headers/body, scope selection, Basic-prefix normalization, TLS/private-network request passthrough, token cache/deduplication, provider auth metadata, static catalog, endpoint selection, onboarding defaults, custom-tool payload adaptation, streamed legacy `function_call` parsing, and failover/context classification.

- If no new test is added, why not: New focused tests were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

OpenClaw now includes a bundled `gigachat` provider with auth choice `gigachat-authorization-key`, env var `GIGACHAT_AUTHORIZATION_KEY`, default model `gigachat/GigaChat-2`, optional plugin config `scope`, optional endpoint selection between `main` and `business`, and custom-tool support through a GigaChat `functions/function_call` payload adapter.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user has Sber Authorization key] -> [no bundled GigaChat provider] -> [manual/custom config only]

After:
[user runs GigaChat onboarding] -> [Authorization key stored] -> [runtime OAuth exchange] -> [Bearer token chat request]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The new provider stores the long-lived Sber Authorization key through the existing provider auth profile path and only keeps short-lived access tokens in runtime memory/cache. OAuth calls use OpenClaw provider HTTP helpers for timeout, SSRF, proxy, and TLS handling, and protected OAuth headers cannot be overridden by configured request headers. GigaChat can now request existing OpenClaw custom tools through the normal tool execution path; this does not add new tools or bypass existing tool policies, and the adapter only rewrites provider wire format.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS Darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: Local source checkout; Node-based Vitest wrapper
- Model/provider: `gigachat/GigaChat-2`, `gigachat/GigaChat-2-Pro`, `gigachat/GigaChat-2-Max`
- Integration/channel (if any): GigaChat provider plugin
- Relevant config (redacted): `GIGACHAT_AUTHORIZATION_KEY` expected for live auth; not present during local verification

### Steps

1. Add the GigaChat plugin package, manifest, provider entry, onboarding, catalog, runtime auth, docs, and labeler/docs navigation updates.
2. Run focused provider and transport tests with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/gigachat/index.test.ts extensions/gigachat/runtime-auth.test.ts extensions/gigachat/stream.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`.
3. Run targeted formatting with `pnpm format:check -- src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts extensions/gigachat/index.ts extensions/gigachat/index.test.ts extensions/gigachat/openclaw.plugin.json extensions/gigachat/stream.ts extensions/gigachat/stream.test.ts docs/providers/gigachat.md .artifacts/gigachat-provider-pr.md`.
4. Run generated docs check with `node scripts/generate-plugin-inventory-doc.mjs --check`.
5. Run whitespace check with `git diff --check`.

### Expected

- GigaChat provider registration, catalog, onboarding defaults, endpoint selection, OAuth exchange, custom-tool payload adaptation, streamed `function_call` parsing, and error classification tests pass.

### Actual

- Focused tests passed: `Test Files 5 passed (5)`, `Tests 349 passed (349)`.
- Targeted format check passed: `All matched files use the correct format`.
- Generated plugin inventory check passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- Live GigaChat smoke and broad remote changed gate were not run due missing credentials/certificate and unavailable Crabbox/Testbox tooling.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Provider registration/auth metadata, static catalog, business endpoint config, onboarding defaults, custom-tool payload adaptation, streamed legacy `function_call` parsing, GigaChat rate/context classification, OAuth token request shape, TLS/private-network passthrough, token caching, concurrent refresh deduplication, and token endpoint error surfacing.
- Edge cases checked: Pasted Authorization key with and without `Basic ` prefix, invalid/missing token response, scoped OAuth body, protected header override filtering, cached token reuse before refresh margin, required tool-choice downgrade, pinned tool-choice mapping, tool-result replay conversion, and object-valued GigaChat function arguments in streamed chunks.
- What you did **not** verify: Real Sber OAuth token exchange, real chat completion, real certificate trust path, and broad Testbox/Crabbox changed gate.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: New users can run `openclaw onboard --auth-choice gigachat-authorization-key` or set `GIGACHAT_AUTHORIZATION_KEY`; optional config lives under `plugins.entries.gigachat.config.scope` and `plugins.entries.gigachat.config.endpoint`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: GigaChat live TLS/certificate setup can fail on machines that do not trust the required Russian root certificate.
  - Mitigation: Docs recommend provider-scoped `models.providers.gigachat.request.tls.ca` so the Russian root certificate applies only to GigaChat OAuth/chat requests; OS trust and `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` are documented as process-wide fallbacks, and `insecureSkipVerify` is discouraged outside controlled development.
- Risk: GigaChat multimodal attachments and built-in functions differ from OpenAI-compatible payloads.
  - Mitigation: Initial catalog is text-only for multimodal input and only adapts OpenClaw custom tools; built-in GigaChat functions remain out of scope.
- Risk: GigaChat streams custom function calls as legacy `delta.function_call`, not OpenAI `delta.tool_calls`.
  - Mitigation: The OpenAI-compatible transport now recognizes legacy `function_call` chunks and tests lock that they become normal OpenClaw `toolCall` blocks with `stopReason: "toolUse"`.
- Risk: OAuth token exchange could create excess token endpoint calls.
  - Mitigation: Runtime auth caches tokens until shortly before expiry and deduplicates concurrent refreshes.

target.title: perf(plugins): cut plugin-system thrash from TUI cold start
target.body: big improvement to plugin loading performance on gateway AND TUI (only TUI local once #84686 is merged) BEFORE AFTER ## Summary Cuts plugin-system work during TUI cold start. Freeze drops from ~32s to ~13–16s; residual cost is dominated by two single-plugin module loads (Codex 2.76s, Anthropic 746ms) that this PR doesn't touch. The work is four core fixes, ranked by single-fix impact: ### 1. Cache-busting — snapshot memo store/lookup keys now match (~7s) Previously the memo computed its lookup key from the persisted index and stored entries under the derived index. Those indexes always differ in volatile fields (`generatedAtMs`, `refreshReason`), so the keys never agreed and every lookup missed. **Hit rate goes from 24% → 98%.** Single biggest commit in the PR. ### 2. Plumbing — `PluginMetadataSnapshot` routed through resolve paths (~2–4s) `resolvePluginProviders`, `isPluginProvidersLoadInFlight`, `resolveOwningPluginIdsForProvider`, and `resolveExternalAuthProfilesWithPlugins` all dispatch to helpers that have a manifestRegistry fast path. They now thread the snapshot through so the fast path actually fires instead of every helper rebuilding the manifest registry from disk. `loadPluginManifestRegistryForInstalledIndex` fires drop 685 → ~10. A second plumb threads `discovery?: PluginDiscoveryResult` through `applyPluginAutoEnable` → `detectPluginAutoEnableCandidates` → `resolvePluginAutoEnableReadiness` → `collectConfiguredChannelIds` → the bundled-channel leaf helpers, so a caller's discovery short-circuits the cascade. Only delivers value because (1) made the snapshot memo actually work. ### 3. LRU capacity (supporting) Memo was single-slot and thrashed when two stable input shapes alternated. Capacity-8 LRU keeps both. Necessary precondition for the cache-busting fix to land cleanly — without LRU, the memo evicts itself. ### 4. Eligibility predicate broadened (supporting) The memo previously only stored derived snapshots whose diagnostics were *exclusively* `persisted-registry-stale-policy`, which discarded ~98% of cold-start derives. Now stores every non-empty derived snapshot. To keep the invalidation surface honest, the cached entry's `watchedFiles` set is widened for derived snapshots to include the derived index's plugin manifest paths; the next lookup re-stats them and re-derives on any change. ## Verification - `pnpm tsgo --noEmit --project tsconfig.core.json` — clean - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.memo.test.ts src/plugins/plugin-registry-snapshot.test.ts src/plugins/providers.test.ts` — 93/93 pass (includes a new regression for derived-only manifest edits) - `pnpm build && pnpm openclaw tui` from a cold process — freeze 32s → 13–16s, snapshot memo hit rate 24% → 98%, per-plugin `package.json` reads ~914 → ~370 Not run: full `pnpm test` / `pnpm check`, sibling-process plugin-install interleaving. ## Notes - Three `wip(perf-trace):` commits are preserved in history so the audit instrumentation is easy to cherry-pick for future startup work. The chore() commit at the tip reverts their effects, so net diff against main contains zero `perfMark`/`perfSpan` calls. - An `installRecords` threading optimization was pulled into [`dallin-perf-install-records-threading`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/tree/dallin-perf-install-records-threading) for a follow-on PR — it cut ~264 sync disk reads but only ~140ms of freeze, so not worth bundling here.

target.title: fix: show all allowFrom entries in channels status --probe
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw channels status --probe` only displays the first two `allowFrom` entries due to `.slice(0, 2)` in `src/commands/channels/status.ts`. This creates a misleading diagnostic signal for users with 3+ entries configured, as reported in #51174.

## Fix

Show up to 5 entries (configurable via `MAX_DISPLAY`), with a `+N more` suffix when the list is longer. This balances readability with completeness.

**Before:** `allow:+491733826756,+491747230583` (silently drops 3rd+ entries)
**After:** `allow:+491733826756,+491747230583,+491736561045` (shows all when ≤5)
**After (6+ entries):** `allow:+491...,+492...,+493...,+494...,+495...,+2 more`

## Changes

- `src/commands/channels/status.ts`: Replace `.slice(0, 2)` with display limit of 5 + overflow indicator

Fixes #51174

target.title: feat(heartbeat): add maxCostPerRun config to cap embedded run cost
target.body:
## Problem

A single embedded run on an expensive model with a large context has no cost bound. Existing fixes (trigger dedup, retry guards) reduce how often runs fire but do not cap individual run cost. See #49823, #3181.

## Solution

New optional config option `maxCostPerRun` (USD float, default: none). Before calling `getReplyFromConfig()` in `runHeartbeatOnce()`, estimated cost is computed from context size and model pricing. If the estimate exceeds the cap, the run returns `{ status: "skipped", reason: "cost-cap-exceeded" }` without invoking the model.

Implemented at the dispatch level so it covers heartbeat, cron, and future trigger types.

## How it works

- Token count estimated from `ctx.Body` character length (chars / 4, conservative)
- Model pricing resolved from the config model catalog (`resolveModelCostConfig`) first, then a hardcoded fallback table, then a conservative $15/M default for unknown models
- Provider-prefixed refs (`openai/gpt-4o`, `openrouter/anthropic/claude-opus-4`) handled via `lastIndexOf("/")` stripping
- Free/local models with `cost.input = 0` in the catalog estimate correctly at $0
- Check inserted between `replyOpts` construction and `getReplyFromConfig` call
- Skipped runs emit a warning log with estimated cost and cap value

## Config example

```yaml
heartbeat:
  every: "120m"
  maxCostPerRun: 0.50
```

## Testing

29 tests (15 unit + 7 integration + 7 edge case):

- Pricing: known models, cheap models, unknown model fallback, empty/single-char prompt, 128K context
- Prefix ordering: gpt-4o vs gpt-4, gpt-4-turbo vs gpt-4, o1-mini vs o1, o3-mini vs o3
- Catalog: catalog hit, catalog miss, zero cost (free model), negative cost, bare model name, provider-prefixed, nested provider prefix
- Case insensitivity
- Integration: cap exceeded (getReplyFromConfig not called), cap within (called), cap unset (backward compat), cap=0 (deny all), negative/NaN/Infinity (ignored), free model + cap=0

## Non-goals

Not a global budget system. Caps individual runs only. Step limits and transcript-based estimation are out of scope.

Fixes #49823
Ref #3181

target.body:
## Summary
- Treat explicit non-secret OAuth markers in `models.json` as effective provider auth in the `models status` auth overview.
- Preserve the existing masked `models.json=marker(...)` display while preventing healthy delegated OAuth setups from showing `effective=missing`.
- Add regression coverage for an `openai` provider configured with `apiKey: "oauth:openai-codex"` and a healthy `openai-codex` OAuth profile.

Fixes #83732

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw models status` reported the `openai` provider row as `effective=missing:missing` even when `models.json` intentionally configured the non-secret delegated OAuth marker `apiKey: "oauth:openai-codex"` and Codex OAuth auth was available.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `fix/models-status-oauth-marker-effective-83732`, using the existing `models status` command Vitest suite.

Exact steps or command run after fix:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/commands/models/list.auth-overview.ts src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts
$ git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix:

```console
✓  commands  ../../src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts (22 tests) 8049ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  22 passed (22)

Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 2.1s on 2 files with 217 rules using 1 threads.
```

Observed result after fix: The regression test now exercises the reported config shape with `openai` using `oauth:openai-codex`, no direct OpenAI env key, and a valid `openai-codex` OAuth profile. The JSON auth overview reports `openai.effective` as `{ kind: "models.json", detail: "marker(oauth:openai-codex)" }`, keeps `missingProvidersInUse` empty, and `--check` exits 0.

What was not tested: I did not run against a live personal auth profile store or call the OpenAI/Codex network. This change is limited to the local `models status` auth overview classification and is covered by command-level tests.

target.title: feat(plugins): add optional api.resetSession()
target.body:
## Summary

`main` already has gateway-side session reset support through `performGatewaySessionReset()`.

This PR adds optional plugin-side `api.resetSession(key, reason?)` support on top of the current mainline architecture.

- Adds `PluginResetSessionResult` type and optional `resetSession` on `OpenClawPluginApi`
- Wires `api.resetSession(...)` in the plugin registry only in full registration mode and only when the gateway exposes `sessions.reset`
- Reuses gateway-owned canonicalization and reset resolution (no plugin-side duplicate logic)
- Unifies config snapshot usage in `performGatewaySessionReset` to eliminate config-drift between target resolution and entry lookup
- Adds per-canonical-key in-flight guard in the reset service
- Exports `PluginResetSessionResult` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`

## Scope

This PR:
- adds `PluginResetSessionResult` to `src/plugins/types.ts`
- adds optional `resetSession` to `OpenClawPluginApi`
- adds builder support in `src/plugins/api-builder.ts`
- wires `api.resetSession(...)` in `src/plugins/registry.ts` (full-mode + capability gated)
- adds optional `existingCfg` param to `loadSessionEntry` in `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`
- adds in-flight guard and unified resolution in `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts`
- exports the result type from `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
- adds focused plugin contract tests (17 tests)
- refreshes generated plugin SDK API baselines

This PR does not:
- change gateway reset RPC behavior
- broaden session architecture scope beyond the plugin API surface
- revive any stale-branch refactor baggage from the closed #43375

## Behavior

`api.resetSession(key, reason?)`:
- accepts `"new"` or `"reset"` reason values (defaults to `"new"`)
- trims and validates the key
- delegates to gateway-owned `performGatewaySessionReset`
- resolves to a structured plugin result instead of rejecting for operational failures
- absent in setup-only, setup-runtime, and cli-metadata registration modes
- absent when gateway does not expose `sessions.reset`

Success: `{ ok: true, key, sessionId }`
Failure: `{ ok: false, key, code, message }`

## Local Verification

```
pnpm test -- src/plugins/reset-session.test.ts     # 17/17 pass
pnpm test -- src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts      # 77/77 pass
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check                           # OK
pnpm tsgo                                           # no new errors
```

Replaces #43375 (closed, stale branch).

target.title: fix(google): omit request config with cached content
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #84919.
- Omits per-request `systemInstruction`, `tools`, and `toolConfig` from Gemini GenerateContent requests when `cachedContent` is used.
- Covers both cached-content entry points: explicit `params.cachedContent` and managed Gemini `cacheRetention` prompt caching.

## Root Cause
OpenClaw had two ways to put `cachedContent` on direct Gemini GenerateContent requests:

- explicit `params.cachedContent`, serialized by the Google transport itself
- managed `cacheRetention`, where the embedded-runner Google prompt-cache wrapper creates a Gemini `cachedContents` resource and patches `cachedContent` into the transport payload

The explicit path sent `cachedContent` while still serializing request-level `systemInstruction`, `tools`, and `toolConfig`. The managed path stripped the request system prompt, but it still let request tools serialize before patching `cachedContent` into the payload. Google requires system instructions and tools/tool config to be set on the cached content resource when `cachedContent` is used; sending them again on GenerateContent can return HTTP 400.

## Fix
- Direct Google transport now treats non-empty `options.cachedContent` as a cached-content request and skips request-level `systemInstruction`, `tools`, and `toolConfig`.
- Managed Gemini prompt caching now includes the request tool declarations and tool config in the Gemini `cachedContents.create` request when tools are present.
- The managed cache key now includes a digest of the cached tool/toolConfig payload, so a cached content resource is not reused across different tool surfaces.
- The managed wrapper now removes both `systemPrompt` and `tools` from the downstream GenerateContent context after it has created or reused the matching cached content.

## Why This Is Safe
Uncached Gemini requests are unchanged: they still send system prompts and tools directly on GenerateContent. Cached Gemini requests still include `contents` and generation settings such as temperature, max tokens, and thinking config.

Security and runtime controls are unchanged for auth resolution, guarded model fetch, request URL/header construction, tool execution policy, and Slack/channel policy. This fix changes provider payload construction and managed cache contents only; it does not rely on prompt text to enforce the policy.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Gemini GenerateContent requests that use managed `cacheRetention: "long"` no longer send per-request `systemInstruction`, `tools`, or `toolConfig` after the wrapper creates a Gemini cached-content resource. Google rejects that combination with:

```text
CachedContent can not be used with GenerateContent request setting system_instruction, tools or tool_config.
```

Real environment tested:
- Host: local macOS checkout.
- OpenClaw checkout under test: PR head after managed-cache fix (`198a42bbc6`).
- Code under test: `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/google-prompt-cache.ts` and `extensions/google/transport-stream.ts`.
- Model/provider: `google/gemini-2.5-flash` via the real Google Gemini API.
- Auth source: trusted OpenClaw runtime dotenv resolved a Gemini API key; secret value was not logged.
- Slack transport attempt: two real Slack bot tokens and one app-level token from local `.env`, with both bots installed in the same workspace/channel; IDs redacted below.

Exact live managed-cache steps after this patch:
1. Called `prepareGooglePromptCacheStreamFn` with `extraParams: { cacheRetention: "long" }` for `google/gemini-2.5-flash`.
2. Invoked the returned managed stream wrapper with a large system prompt, one tool declaration, and `toolChoice: "auto"`.
3. Let OpenClaw create the Gemini `cachedContents` resource through the managed prompt-cache path, not by pre-supplying `params.cachedContent`.
4. Captured the managed wrapper's downstream context and final Google transport payload shape.
5. Verified Google accepted the GenerateContent request, returned a model response, and reported `cachedContentTokenCount` mapped to `cacheRead`.
6. Deleted the Gemini `cachedContents` resource after the run.

Redacted live managed-cache log excerpt:

```text
managed cache input:
  extraParams.cacheRetention: long
  wrapperCreated: true
  request context before wrapper: systemPrompt present, tools present, toolChoice=auto

managed cachedContents result:
  entryStatus: ready
  cachedContent prefix: cachedContents
  cacheRetention: long
  cacheConfigDigest present: true
  cacheConfigDigest length: 263

managed wrapper downstream context after cache creation:
  systemPrompt present: false
  tools present: false
  options.cacheRetention: null
  options.toolChoice: auto

OpenClaw GenerateContent payload shape after managed wrapper patch:
  cachedContent: present
  systemInstruction key present: false
  tools key present: false
  toolConfig key present: false
  generationConfig key present: true

GenerateContent result:
  ok: true
  stopReason: stop
  contentTypes: text
  response text marker: OPENCLAW_GEMINI_MANAGED_CACHE_14KU17
  usage.input: 26
  usage.output: 17
  usage.cacheRead: 8569
  usage.cacheWrite: 0
  usage.totalTokens: 8612

cleanup:
  deletedCachedContent: true
  delete status: 200
```

Observed result after fix: the real managed `cacheRetention: "long"` path created a cached-content resource, patched `cachedContent` onto the GenerateContent payload, omitted all three conflicting request-level keys, and received a successful Gemini response with `cacheRead=8569`.

Additional explicit `cachedContent` proof:

```text
create cachedContents request shape:
  model: models/gemini-2.5-flash
  contents: present, large stable cached prefix
  systemInstruction: present at cache creation
  tools: present at cache creation
  toolConfig: present at cache creation
  ttl: 300s

OpenClaw GenerateContent payload shape after patch:
  cachedContent: present
  systemInstruction key present: false
  tools key present: false
  toolConfig key present: false
  generationConfig key present: true

GenerateContent result:
  ok: true
  stopReason: stop
  usage.cacheRead: 6990
  cleanup.deletedCachedContent: true
```

Slack two-bot proof attempt:

```text
SLACK_CHANNEL_ID present: true
Slack app token present: true
slack_token1: auth.ok=true team=<redacted> user=<redacted> bot=<redacted>
slack_token1: channel.ok=true isMember=true isChannel=true
slack_token3: auth.ok=true team=<redacted> user=<redacted> bot=<redacted>
slack_token3: channel.ok=true isMember=true isChannel=true
```

Command shape:

```text
OPENCLAW_QA_SLACK_CHANNEL_ID=<redacted channel id>
OPENCLAW_QA_SLACK_DRIVER_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted xoxb for driver bot>
OPENCLAW_QA_SLACK_SUT_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted xoxb for SUT bot>
OPENCLAW_QA_SLACK_SUT_APP_TOKEN=<redacted xapp token>
OPENCLAW_QA_SLACK_CAPTURE_CONTENT=1
OPENCLAW_QA_REDACT_PUBLIC_METADATA=0
pnpm openclaw qa slack \
  --credential-source env \
  --scenario slack-canary \
  --provider-mode live-frontier \
  --model google/gemini-2.5-flash \
  --alt-model google/gemini-2.5-flash \
  --output-dir .artifacts/slack-gemini-live-proof-20260521T132154Z
```

Slack observed behavior:
- The driver bot posted two real Slack messages into the configured channel asking the SUT bot to reply with unique `SLACK_QA_ECHO_*` markers.
- The lane failed with `timed out after 45000ms waiting for Slack message; retried 1x` and `slack-qa-observed-messages.json` was empty.

The Slack attempt did not produce a successful reply, so it is recorded as a transport-path attempt rather than the passing provider proof. The passing live managed Gemini proof above exercises the reported `cacheRetention` provider path directly.

## Tests
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/google-prompt-cache.test.ts extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts -- -t "google prompt cache|cachedContent|guarded fetch transport"`
- `git diff --check`
- IDE lints for changed files: no diagnostics

## Out Of Scope
- Changing Gemini cache TTL or refresh timing.
- Changing Slack/Socket Mode handling or two-bot QA harness behavior.
- Adding compatibility fallbacks that send dynamic tools or system prompts alongside `cachedContent`.

## Transparency
AI-assisted change; locally reviewed and validated with focused tests plus live managed Gemini provider proof.

target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Refs #77969
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

TTS can turn emoji and decorative symbols into noisy spoken names. Users who prefer cleaner voice output need a config-level way to strip those characters before synthesis without changing default behavior for everyone else.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `tts-skip-v3`, commit `06545b841d08`, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (`extensions/microsoft` -> `node-edge-tts` -> `speech.platform.bing.com`). No API keys or private account secrets were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... register microsoft plugin, call textToSpeech twice with skipEmojiSymbols=false/true, then call an emoji-only enabled case ... TS`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- After-fix evidence:
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # Registers the bundled Microsoft Speech plugin and performs real synthesis
  # through node-edge-tts / speech.platform.bing.com after this patch.
  TS
  {
    "branch": "tts-skip-v3",
    "commit": "06545b841d08",
    "provider": "microsoft",
    "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
    "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
    "observed": [
      {
        "name": "disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": false,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false.mp3",
        "bytes": 39456,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      },
      {
        "name": "enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": true,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true.mp3",
        "bytes": 36288,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      }
    ],
    "emptyAfterStrip": {
      "success": false,
      "error": "TTS text is empty after removing emoji and symbol characters",
      "provider": null
    },
    "artifactsDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof"
  }

  $ ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration,size ...
  ffprobe was not installed in this container, so artifact validation used byte size plus MP3 frame header checks.

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       32 passed (32)

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       14 passed (14)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing `✓`, `©`, and `🫠`; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same speech-core preprocessing path that all providers share.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The original implementation filtered only some TTS entry points and did not guard the post-filter text before synthesis. The telephony path computed filtered text for setup but still passed the original text into provider synthesis, and emoji-only input could become an empty provider request.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
  - stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
  - telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can enable `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` to strip emoji/symbol characters before speech synthesis. Unset or `false` preserves existing behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
text -> effective TTS config -> optional emoji/symbol strip -> empty guard -> provider synthesis
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: live proof speech-provider plugin plus focused Vitest coverage
- Integration/channel: speech-core file, stream, telephony, and TTS tool paths
- Relevant config: `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols=true|false`

### Steps

1. Run the live proof script shown above.
2. Run focused speech-core tests.
3. Run focused TTS tool tests.
4. Run test and production type gates.

### Expected

Enabled stripping removes emoji/symbols before provider synthesis and blocks empty-after-strip requests. Disabled mode preserves original text.

### Actual

The proof and tests match the expected behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Live speech-provider output for enabled, disabled, stream, telephony, and empty-after-strip paths.
- Focused speech-core test output.
- Focused TTS tool test output.
- Test and production typecheck output.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: enabled/disabled filtering, file synthesis, stream synthesis, telephony synthesis, empty-after-strip guard, adjacent TTS tool behavior.
- Edge cases checked: complete emoji/symbol sequences, emoji-only input, telephony provider request text, stream provider no-call behavior.
- Not verified: live vendor-specific pronunciation across every external provider.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: optional new config `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols`; no change required when unset.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Unicode stripping removes characters a user wanted spoken.
- Mitigation: behavior is opt-in and disabled by default.
- Risk: filtering differs across TTS paths.
- Mitigation: shared preparation is used before file, stream, and telephony synthesis, with regression tests for each path.

target.title: perf(plugins): cut plugin-system thrash from TUI cold start
target.body: big improvement to plugin loading performance on gateway AND TUI (only TUI local once #84686 is merged) BEFORE AFTER ## Summary Cuts plugin-system work during TUI cold start. Freeze drops from ~32s to ~13–16s; residual cost is dominated by two single-plugin module loads (Codex 2.76s, Anthropic 746ms) that this PR doesn't touch. The work is four core fixes, ranked by single-fix impact: ### 1. Cache-busting — snapshot memo store/lookup keys now match (~7s) Previously the memo computed its lookup key from the persisted index and stored entries under the derived index. Those indexes always differ in volatile fields (`generatedAtMs`, `refreshReason`), so the keys never agreed and every lookup missed. **Hit rate goes from 24% → 98%.** Single biggest commit in the PR. ### 2. Plumbing — `PluginMetadataSnapshot` routed through resolve paths (~2–4s) `resolvePluginProviders`, `isPluginProvidersLoadInFlight`, `resolveOwningPluginIdsForProvider`, and `resolveExternalAuthProfilesWithPlugins` all dispatch to helpers that have a manifestRegistry fast path. They now thread the snapshot through so the fast path actually fires instead of every helper rebuilding the manifest registry from disk. `loadPluginManifestRegistryForInstalledIndex` fires drop 685 → ~10. A second plumb threads `discovery?: PluginDiscoveryResult` through `applyPluginAutoEnable` → `detectPluginAutoEnableCandidates` → `resolvePluginAutoEnableReadiness` → `collectConfiguredChannelIds` → the bundled-channel leaf helpers, so a caller's discovery short-circuits the cascade. Only delivers value because (1) made the snapshot memo actually work. ### 3. LRU capacity (supporting) Memo was single-slot and thrashed when two stable input shapes alternated. Capacity-8 LRU keeps both. Necessary precondition for the cache-busting fix to land cleanly — without LRU, the memo evicts itself. ### 4. Eligibility predicate broadened (supporting) The memo previously only stored derived snapshots whose diagnostics were *exclusively* `persisted-registry-stale-policy`, which discarded ~98% of cold-start derives. Now stores every non-empty derived snapshot. To keep the invalidation surface honest, the cached entry's `watchedFiles` set is widened for derived snapshots to include the derived index's plugin manifest paths; the next lookup re-stats them and re-derives on any change. ## Verification - `pnpm tsgo --noEmit --project tsconfig.core.json` — clean - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.memo.test.ts src/plugins/plugin-registry-snapshot.test.ts src/plugins/providers.test.ts` — 93/93 pass (includes a new regression for derived-only manifest edits) - `pnpm build && pnpm openclaw tui` from a cold process — freeze 32s → 13–16s, snapshot memo hit rate 24% → 98%, per-plugin `package.json` reads ~914 → ~370 Not run: full `pnpm test` / `pnpm check`, sibling-process plugin-install interleaving. ## Notes - Three `wip(perf-trace):` commits are preserved in history so the audit instrumentation is easy to cherry-pick for future startup work. The chore() commit at the tip reverts their effects, so net diff against main contains zero `perfMark`/`perfSpan` calls. - An `installRecords` threading optimization was pulled into [`dallin-perf-install-records-threading`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/tree/dallin-perf-install-records-threading) for a follow-on PR — it cut ~264 sync disk reads but only ~140ms of freeze, so not worth bundling here.

target.title: fix(models): report oauth markers as effective auth
target.body:
## Summary
- Treat explicit non-secret OAuth markers in `models.json` as effective provider auth in the `models status` auth overview.
- Preserve the existing masked `models.json=marker(...)` display while preventing healthy delegated OAuth setups from showing `effective=missing`.
- Add regression coverage for an `openai` provider configured with `apiKey: "oauth:openai-codex"` and a healthy `openai-codex` OAuth profile.

Fixes #83732

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw models status` reported the `openai` provider row as `effective=missing:missing` even when `models.json` intentionally configured the non-secret delegated OAuth marker `apiKey: "oauth:openai-codex"` and Codex OAuth auth was available.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `fix/models-status-oauth-marker-effective-83732`, using the existing `models status` command Vitest suite.

Exact steps or command run after fix:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/commands/models/list.auth-overview.ts src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts
$ git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix:

```console
✓  commands  ../../src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts (22 tests) 8049ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  22 passed (22)

Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 2.1s on 2 files with 217 rules using 1 threads.
```

Observed result after fix: The regression test now exercises the reported config shape with `openai` using `oauth:openai-codex`, no direct OpenAI env key, and a valid `openai-codex` OAuth profile. The JSON auth overview reports `openai.effective` as `{ kind: "models.json", detail: "marker(oauth:openai-codex)" }`, keeps `missingProvidersInUse` empty, and `--check` exits 0.

What was not tested: I did not run against a live personal auth profile store or call the OpenAI/Codex network. This change is limited to the local `models status` auth overview classification and is covered by command-level tests.

## Test plan
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/commands/models/list.auth-overview.ts src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Duplicate check
- Checked open PRs for `83732`, `"oauth:openai-codex" "effective=missing"`, `"oauth delegation markers" "effective auth"`, `"marker(oauth:openai-codex)" "models status"`, and `"models.json" "marker(oauth:openai-codex)" "effective"` immediately before publishing.
